LaPael applies input-dependent noise to early layers of large language models during fine-tuning to generate diverse, semantically consistent paraphrases of training data in latent space. The noised activations flow through the model's remaining layers, creating augmented training examples without external paraphrase generation. Information crosses the model boundary as modified hidden-state activations.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LaPael improves knowledge injection over standard fine-tuning and existing noise-based approaches
claim c2 [qualitative]: Latent-level paraphrasing method applies input-dependent noise to early LLM layers to enable diverse and semantically consistent augmentations directly within the model
claim c3 [qualitative]: LaPael eliminates the recurring costs of paraphrase generation for each knowledge update
claim c4 [qualitative]: Combining LaPael with data-level paraphrasing further enhances performance

Improving Steering Vectors by Targeting Sparse Autoencoder Features (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, sae-feature-targeting, effect-measurement, residual-stream-injection, interpretability-steering
SAE-Targeted Steering (SAE-TS) is a steering method that operates by first learning a linear effect approximator mapping steering vectors to their effects on SAE feature activations. The system measures steering effects by (1) generating text rollouts from a steered model (steering vector added to residual stream at layer 12), (2) passing rollouts back through the model to extract SAE feature activations, and (3) computing mean feature activation differences between steered and unsteered outputs. A linear approximator is trained on 50,000 steering vectors and their measured effects. To construct a targeted steering vector for a desired feature, the method extracts the target feature's decoder column from the learned effect matrix, subtracts a bias term, and normalizes. An adaptive scaling factor is selected per vector to raise cross-entropy loss by 0.5 above baseline.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE-TS balances steering effects with coherence better than CAA and SAE feature steering, when evaluated on a range of tasks
claim c2 [quantitative]: SAE-TS outperforms baseline methods on 7 of 9 steering tasks when measured at peak Behavioral*Coherence score
claim c3 [quantitative]: On the London task, SAE-TS achieves Behavioral*Coherence score of 0.5380 at peak, significantly outperforming CAA (0.0476) and SAE steering (0.0061)
claim c4 [negative]: At high scaling factors well above optimal scale on the London task, SAE-TS exhibits an interesting failure mode where output diversity collapses and the model produces text focused entirely on fashion shows and art exhibitions in London
claim c5 [negative]: All investigations were performed using Gemma-2 2B and 9B, both sharing the same distilled model architecture, so results may not transfer to other models
claim c6 [negative]: SAE-TS can only steer towards features present in the SAE used to measure feature effects; some concepts do not appear as individual SAE features (feature splitting), and niche features may only appear in extremely large SAEs
claim c7 [quantitative]: On Gemma-2-9B, SAE-TS achieves average Behavioral*Coherence score of 0.3376 across 9 tasks, higher than CAA (0.2531) and SAE steering (0.2184); however CAA beats SAE-TS on 4 of 9 tasks and ties on 1 task
claim c8 [qualitative]: The feature effects measurement method uses SAEs to understand the causal effect of steering vector interventions by measuring changes in SAE feature activations between steered and unsteered model outputs

Language Models are Hidden Reasoners: Unlocking Latent Reasoning Capabilities via Self-Rewarding (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-variable-inference, self-rewarding, policy-gradient, variational-optimization, reinforcement-learning, reasoning-compression
LaTRO formulates reasoning as sampling from a latent distribution and optimizes it via a variational framework. A single LLM θ jointly learns to generate reasoning rationales (the 'reasoner') and to evaluate their quality via self-generated reward signals (the 'reward function'). The model conditions on a query, samples multiple reasoning trajectories internally, estimates their value by the log-likelihood of the correct answer following that trajectory, and updates parameters to favor high-quality rationales while maintaining KL regularization to a frozen reference model θ_ref. Information flow: query → sample reasoning rationale → compute likelihood of correct answer given rationale → gradient update on both rationale generation and answer prediction.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On GSM8K, LaTRO improves zero-shot accuracy by an average of 12.5% over base models across Phi-3.5-mini, Mistral-7B, and Llama-3.1-8B with greedy decoding
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K, LaTRO improves zero-shot accuracy by an average of 9.6% over supervised fine-tuning across Phi-3.5-mini, Mistral-7B, and Llama-3.1-8B with greedy decoding
claim c3 [qualitative]: On GSM8K with self-consistency (maj@8), LaTRO achieves 90.5% accuracy for Phi-3.5, 73.8% for Mistral-7B, and 87.0% for Llama-3.1-8B
claim c4 [quantitative]: On ARC-Challenge with greedy decoding, LaTRO improves over base models by up to 2.9% with an average increase of 1.9%
claim c5 [qualitative]: LaTRO enables LLMs to concurrently improve both their reasoning process and ability to evaluate reasoning quality, without requiring external feedback or reward models
claim c6 [qualitative]: LaTRO can train LLMs to generate more concise rationales; when LaTRO is trained with maximum token length of 200, it significantly improves performance under this constraint compared to models trained with longer limits
claim c7 [qualitative]: LaTRO equipped with self-consistency can further enhance performance even with LaTRO's focus on training-time optimization; inference-time scaling remains beneficial
claim c8 [negative]: For ARC-Challenge, LaTRO shows smaller improvements compared to GSM8K; the models are already good at producing the answer either directly or through CoT prompting, hence further optimization of the reasoning process did not yield significant improvement

Constrain Alignment with Sparse Autoencoders (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, preference-optimization, offline-reference, feature-level-constraint, sparsity-enforced, kl-approximation
FPO (Feature-level constrained Preference Optimization) aligns large language models with human preferences by combining Direct Preference Optimization with feature-level constraints derived from pre-trained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). The system computes a preference loss using length-normalized log probability differences and an adaptive margin from a reference model, augmented with MSE constraints on sparse feature activations pooled across tokens. The reference model is computed offline and cached to avoid runtime memory overhead. Information flow: preference pairs → policy and reference forward passes → SAE sparse activations (pre-cached and computed offline) → MSE discrepancy between pooled top-k activations → combined loss with preference term.
claim c1 [quantitative]: FPO achieves up to 5.08% absolute improvement in win rate compared to state-of-the-art baselines on AlpacaEval-2 and Arena-Hard benchmarks
claim c2 [qualitative]: FPO achieves approximately 17.6% GPU memory reduction compared to TDPO-2 during training
claim c3 [quantitative]: FPO achieves 64.1% training accuracy on UltraFeedback dataset with entropy of 1.68, compared to TDPO-2 at 64.2% accuracy and 1.68 entropy
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Gemma-2-2B, FPO achieves +0.5 score improvement on MT-Bench compared to SFT baseline
claim c5 [qualitative]: FPO leverages pre-trained Sparse Autoencoders to introduce feature-level constraints, where only a few dozen out of 16,000 features are active at any given time, enhancing computational efficiency
claim c6 [qualitative]: FPO uses offline reference caching to avoid loading the reference model during training, requiring only minimal I/O overhead to read pre-stored one-dimensional tensors at each step
claim c7 [qualitative]: FPO enables precise fine-grained control over specific model capabilities (instruction following, multilingual output, safety, sentiment) by selectively adjusting feature-level constraint weights (λ) on domain-specific features
claim c8 [negative]: FPO improvements on Gemma-2-9B are limited compared to Gemma-2-2B, attributed to larger models achieving more thorough feature decomposition with the same SAE width, filtering more noisy features and leading to less room for improvement

RESOLVE: Relational Reasoning with Symbolic and Object-Level Features Using Vector Symbolic Processing (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: vector-symbolic-architecture, hd-attention, binding-operation, bundling, relational-reasoning, bipolar-representation
RESOLVE is a neuro-vector symbolic architecture that combines object-level features with relational representations in high-dimensional space. Information flows as follows: input objects are encoded to low-dimensional embeddings, then mapped to high-dimensional space via an HD-Encoder (single-dimension convolution with learnable HD-Basis vectors). A novel HD-Attention mechanism computes relational scores using bipolar cosine similarity in this high-dimensional space. The attention weights mix HD-encoded objects via bundling (summation). Learnable symbolic vectors representing relations are also mapped to HD space and bound (Hadamard product/element-wise multiplication) with the mixed object vectors, allowing relational and object-level information to coexist with minimal interference. The resulting high-dimensional vectors are then projected back to low-dimensional space via a learnable linear layer and passed to attentional decoders.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RESOLVE is the first to propose a strategy for addressing the relational bottleneck problem using a vector symbolic architecture, capturing relational representations of input objects in a hyperdimensional vector space while maintaining object-level information in the same representation structure with minimized interference
claim c2 [quantitative]: On the order relations task (learning asymmetric relations from limited examples), RESOLVE achieves over 90% accuracy with just 210 training samples, performing better than the second best model and better than Abstractor
claim c3 [qualitative]: On the SET task with pre-processed objects, RESOLVE outperforms all baselines up to x better than the second best model and x better than the Abstractor
claim c4 [quantitative]: On the SET task with low-processed objects, RESOLVE outperforms state of the art with more than 92% accuracy using just 600 training samples
claim c5 [qualitative]: On MNIST-Math (extracting non-linear weighted subtraction rule from digit image pairs), RESOLVE outperforms other baselines with better accuracy than the transformer and better accuracy than the Abstractor
claim c6 [qualitative]: On 6-element sequence sorting (purely relational sequence-to-sequence task), RESOLVE achieves better accuracy than baselines (x to x better than Relational-Abstractor) on element-wise accuracy metric
claim c7 [quantitative]: On mathematical reasoning (partially-relational sequence-to-sequence: Numbers_place_value, Comparison_pair, Comparison_closest), RESOLVE achieves 41.86% overall accuracy (average across three task types and 100–10k training set sizes), outperforming state-of-the-art, and achieves higher accuracy with small training sets
claim c8 [qualitative]: The HD-Attention mechanism has better computational performance compared to self-attention with higher memory bandwidth utilization due to use of bipolar HD representation and operations such as bundling and binding

Analogical Reasoning Within a Conceptual Hyperspace (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: hyperdimensional-computing, vector-symbolic-architecture, conceptual-spaces, analogical-reasoning, radial-basis-kernels, resonator-networks, fractional-power-encoding
A neuro-symbolic system that combines complex-sampled hyperdimensional computing (HDC) with Conceptual Spaces Theory (CST) to perform analogical reasoning. Concepts are encoded as high-dimensional complex hypervectors via fractional power encoding (FPE) of normalized property values along domain basis dimensions, creating 3D radial basis kernel functions embedded in ~10,000-dimensional space. Analogical mapping is solved by binding encoded hypervectors according to geometric models (e.g., parallelogram method for category-based analogy), then using resonator networks to factorize the bound superposition and recover prototype locations in the original property space. The system bridges symbolic and connectionist representations through HDC's ability to represent both discrete symbolic structures and continuous geometric relationships.
claim c1 [qualitative]: HDC operationalizes Conceptual Spaces Theory by simultaneously supporting sensory observation encoding, algebraic calculus over distance metrics, logical calculus, concept cross-referencing across sensory/geometric/symbolic processing, and associative long-term memory interaction.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Complex-sampled HDC affords all other sample types (binary, bipolar, real) and is the most computationally powerful for analogical reasoning, with a tradeoff between processing speed and computational power.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The proposed architecture solves category-based analogies (e.g., PURPLE:BLUE::ORANGE:?) using the parallelogram model implemented via binding operations on encoded hypervectors.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The architecture solves property-based analogies by identifying salient property dimensions via distance metrics (inner product between hypervectors) and finding closest prototype locations within those dimensions.
claim c5 [quantitative]: In a toy color domain analogy experiment (PURPLE:BLUE::ORANGE:YELLOW), the resonator network decoded the correct target prototype location in two iterations using a codebook of 41 discretized locations per dimension (normalized range [-10, 10] at 0.5 increments).
claim c6 [qualitative]: Resonator networks factor bound superposition in time scaling linearly with search space size, enabling faster factorization than any other known method.
claim c7 [negative]: The origin and hierarchical structure of property dimensions remain deeply ungrounded in theory; long-term memory implementation for storing and retrieving prototypes and symbolic data structures (labels, category relationships) is beyond the scope of this work and remains a work in progress.
claim c8 [negative]: For the toy model presented, the neurally-plausible conceptual hyperspace is more complex than a traditional approach using base-10 numeric vectors; engineering tractability at scale remains undemonstrated.

IntentGPT: Few-shot Intent Discovery with Large Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: in-context-learning, few-shot-prompting, prompt-generation, semantic-sampling, intent-discovery, known-intent-feedback
IntentGPT is a training-free system for few-shot intent discovery that chains two frozen LLMs: an In-Context Prompt Generator (ICPG) that creates a task-specific prompt from labeled training examples, and an Intent Predictor that uses the generated prompt plus semantically-sampled few-shot examples and a dynamically-updated list of known/discovered intents to classify user utterances and discover new intents during inference. Known Intent Feedback loops discovered intents back into the prompt for online learning without parameter updates.
claim c1 [quantitative]: IntentGPT-4 in the 50-shot setting outperforms prior methods in both unsupervised and semi-supervised scenarios on CLINC, achieving ACC of 88.76
claim c2 [quantitative]: IntentGPT-4 in the 50-shot setting outperforms prior methods on BANKING, achieving ACC of 77.21 vs. prior best
claim c3 [quantitative]: IntentGPT-4 achieves ARI of 84.76 on CLINC in the 50-shot setting
claim c4 [quantitative]: IntentGPT-4 achieves ARI of 66.66 on BANKING in the 50-shot setting
claim c5 [qualitative]: IntentGPT even in the 0-shot unsupervised setting outperforms all previous unsupervised methods
claim c6 [qualitative]: Known Intent Feedback (KIF) is essential: without it, the number of discovered intents becomes uncontrolled, indicating the model cannot reuse known intents when not explicitly aware of them
claim c7 [negative]: Semantic Known Intent Feedback Sampling (SKIF) does not result in significant gains; optimal performance includes all known intents in the prompt
claim c8 [negative]: Strong restriction on context length of LLM; computational and monetary costs increase with context; privacy and security concerns from using external LLM APIs like GPT-4; Llama-2 offers cost-effective local alternative but may have bias concerns

Search, Verify and Feedback: Towards Next Generation Post-training Paradigm of Foundation Models via Verifier Engineering (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: verifier-engineering, search-verify-feedback, survey, post-training-paradigm, gc-mdp, automated-verification
This is a survey and framework paper proposing 'verifier engineering' as a post-training paradigm for foundation models. The system itself is conceptual—a three-stage pipeline (Search, Verify, Feedback) that formalizes how to generate candidate outputs, evaluate them using automated verifiers, and incorporate verification signals to improve model behavior. Information flows from sampled model outputs through verifier evaluation (ranking, scoring, binary, or text feedback) into downstream training (SFT, preference learning, RL) or inference-time modifications. The paper models this as a Goal-Conditioned MDP and provides a taxonomy of existing methods across these three stages.
claim c1 [qualitative]: verifier engineering constitutes a fundamental pathway toward achieving Artificial General Intelligence
claim c2 [qualitative]: verifier engineering extends the construction of supervision signals beyond traditional manual feature extraction and data annotation by utilizing a suite of effective automated verifiers to perform verification tasks and provide meaningful feedback
claim c3 [qualitative]: verifier engineering shifts improvement of foundation models from a data-centric endeavor to a systematic engineering challenge emphasizing design and orchestration of complex verification systems
claim c4 [qualitative]: fine-grained verification is preferable from a machine learning perspective as it mitigates the risks of shortcut learning and bias associated with coarse-grained verification, thereby enhancing generalization
claim c5 [qualitative]: tree search significantly improves the likelihood of discovering global optimal solution, particularly in environments with complex state spaces, by simultaneously considering multiple paths and mitigating the risk of being locked into sub-optimal decisions made early on
claim c6 [qualitative]: model-based verifiers adapt to varying contexts and tasks through learning, allowing for more nuanced and context-sensitive evaluations, but introduce an element of uncertainty and can require significant training and computational resources
claim c7 [qualitative]: the central challenge in the current era is determining how to supply more effective supervision signals to foundation models to achieve general artificial intelligence capabilities, as traditional data engineering approaches have reached practical limitations
claim c8 [theoretical]: verifier engineering can be formalized as a Goal-Conditioned Markov Decision Process (GC-MDP) where search corresponds to action selection, verify maps to the reward function evaluating alignment with specified goals, and feedback relates to policy distribution optimization

Do I Know This Entity? Knowledge Awareness and Hallucinations in Language Models (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-steering, interpretability, hallucination, entity-recognition, knowledge-awareness, mechanistic-analysis
The paper uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) trained on model activations to interpret internal representations in language models. SAEs decompose residual stream activations into sparse linear combinations of learned dictionary elements. The system identifies specific SAE latent directions that activate selectively on known vs. unknown entities by analyzing activation patterns on the final token of entity mentions. These latent directions are then causally manipulated via activation steering to steer model behavior (refusal vs. hallucination). The paper also analyzes how these directions causally affect downstream attention mechanisms involved in attribute extraction.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Using sparse autoencoders, SAE latent directions are discovered that encode knowledge awareness: detecting whether the model recognizes an entity and can recall facts about it
claim c2 [qualitative]: Entity recognition latents generalize across diverse entity types (players, films, songs, cities) with higher minimum separation scores in middle layers indicating entity-type-agnostic features
claim c3 [quantitative]: Steering with the unknown entity latent induces almost 100% refusal rate across all entity types in Gemma 2 2B when tested on 100 questions about unknown entities
claim c4 [qualitative]: Weight orthogonalization of the top unknown entity direction on every output matrix results in large reduction in refusal responses, demonstrating the direction plays a crucial role in knowledge refusal
claim c5 [qualitative]: Entity recognition latents causally regulate attention to entity tokens in downstream attention heads; steering with unknown entity latent reduces attention to entity even in prompts with known entities
claim c6 [quantitative]: An uncertainty latent in Gemma 2B IT layer 13 achieves 73.2 AUROC score and 72 F1 score as a classifier distinguishing correct versus incorrect responses on entity attribute questions
claim c7 [qualitative]: Despite SAEs being trained on the base model, entity recognition latent directions have causal effect on the chat model's refusal behavior, suggesting chat finetuning repurposes existing mechanisms
claim c8 [negative]: The methodology for labeling entities as 'known' or 'unknown' might introduce labeling inaccuracies, as the model could guess attributes despite not knowing about the entity or fail to recall specific attributes while knowing about the entity

XGrammar: Flexible and Efficient Structured Generation Engine for Large Language Models (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, context-free-grammar, pushdown-automata, token-mask-cache, cpu-gpu-overlap
XGrammar is a constrained decoding engine for LLMs that enforces context-free grammar (CFG) compliance during token generation. It represents CFGs as byte-level pushdown automata (PDA) and accelerates mask generation by partitioning tokens into context-independent (prechecked during preprocessing) and context-dependent (resolved at runtime). The system maintains an adaptive token mask cache indexed by PDA stack state, executes context-dependent token validation via a persistent execution stack supporting fast branching and rollback, and co-schedules mask generation on CPU with LLM inference on GPU to overlap computation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: XGrammar achieves up to 100x speedup over existing solutions in per-token latency for context-free grammar
claim c2 [quantitative]: XGrammar-integrated LLM serving engine achieves up to 80x speedup in end-to-end LLM serving with structured output on H100 GPU
claim c3 [quantitative]: Context-dependent tokens account for less than 1% (1134 out of 128k) for Llama-3.1 model using JSON grammar
claim c4 [quantitative]: Adaptive storage method reduces total memory usage to 0.2% (from 160 MB to 0.46 MB) for Llama-3.1 model and JSON grammar
claim c5 [quantitative]: Context expansion reduces context-dependent tokens by 90% (from 1134 to 120) for Llama-3.1 model and JSON grammar
claim c6 [quantitative]: Persistent execution stack with rollback reduces the number of characters checked across entire vocabulary to 30% for Llama-3.1 model and JSON grammar
claim c7 [quantitative]: Adaptive token mask cache optimization yields 248.6x speedup in per-token latency; cumulative with node merging (1.7x), rule inlining (4.4x), and context expansion (1.9x) achieves 65.776 ms to 0.018 ms reduction
claim c8 [quantitative]: XGrammar ensures 100% syntactic correctness on function calling (JSON schema) task vs. 62% without constraint, and 100% on XML code generation vs. 80% without constraint

Incremental Sentence Processing Mechanisms in Autoregressive Transformer Language Models (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, circuit-analysis, causal-intervention, garden-path-sentences, feature-attribution, syntactic-processing
The paper uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on autoregressive transformer language model activations to decompose hidden states into interpretable features. These features are assembled into causal circuits using attribution patching with integrated gradients (AtP-IG) to identify which features causally influence the model's preference for garden-path vs. non-garden-path sentence continuations. Information flows from embeddings through residual stream and attention/MLP features to output logits, with causal relevance determined by indirect effect on the probability difference between competing readings.
claim c1 [qualitative]: While many important features LMs use to process garden path sentences relate to syntactic structure, some reflect syntactically irrelevant heuristics
claim c2 [qualitative]: Many of the most important features LMs use are interpretable and syntax-related; however, some uninterpretable or spurious features exist
claim c3 [qualitative]: LMs' representations encode multiple interpretations of ambiguous garden path sentences simultaneously, with both pro-GP and anti-GP features having non-zero average activations
claim c4 [qualitative]: Causal interventions on subject and object feature detectors reverse the model's garden-path reading preference, while random feature interventions do not
claim c5 [qualitative]: LMs do not re-use features from garden path sentence processing to answer follow-up questions; feature overlap (IoU) between initial reading and GPRC circuits is 0% for NP/S and 0.2% for NP/Z
claim c6 [qualitative]: LMs do not repair their initial incorrect representations via reanalysis when answering follow-up questions; they generate new features that are not syntactic
claim c7 [negative]: Circuit faithfulness values of 0.20 (NP/S) and 3.48 (NP/Z) indicate that the identified features do not capture the complete mechanism; many important features remain unidentified
claim c8 [qualitative]: Pythia-70m shows garden-path biases in NP/Z and MV/RR sentence structures but prefers non-garden-path continuations for NP/S sentences

Training Large Language Models to Reason in a Continuous Latent Space (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning-loop, continuous-thought-injection, multi-stage-curriculum, breadth-first-search-emergence, language-to-latent-transition
Coconut (Chain of Continuous Thought) modifies standard LLM decoding to reason in latent space rather than language space. During reasoning, the model switches between 'language mode' (standard autoregressive token generation) and 'latent mode' (feeding the last hidden state directly as the next input embedding, bypassing the language model head and embedding layer). Special tokens <bot> and <eot> mark latent reasoning boundaries. A multi-stage training curriculum gradually replaces language reasoning chain tokens with continuous thought vectors, supervising latent reasoning with language CoT data. The continuous thoughts remain fully differentiable and support backpropagation. At inference, the model may output final answers in language or maintain continuous latent reasoning steps for planning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Continuous thoughts can encode multiple alternative next reasoning steps, allowing the model to perform a breadth-first search (BFS) to solve the problem, rather than prematurely committing to a single deterministic path like CoT
claim c2 [quantitative]: Coconut outperforms CoT on ProsQA logical reasoning task requiring substantial planning ability
claim c3 [quantitative]: Coconut achieves comparable accuracy to CoT on ProntoQA logical reasoning while generating significantly fewer tokens
claim c4 [quantitative]: Coconut generates 9.0 tokens on ProntoQA versus 92.5 tokens for CoT while maintaining comparable accuracy
claim c5 [quantitative]: Coconut generates 14.2 tokens on ProsQA versus 49.4 tokens for CoT while achieving higher accuracy (97.0% vs 77.5%)
claim c6 [quantitative]: On GSM8k math reasoning, Coconut achieves 34.1% accuracy with 8.2 tokens, outperforming No-CoT (16.5% accuracy) while generating fewer tokens than CoT (42.9% accuracy with 25.0 tokens)
claim c7 [negative]: Coconut trained without multi-stage curriculum (w/o curriculum) performs no better than No-CoT baseline, achieving only 14.4% accuracy on GSM8k versus 34.1% with curriculum
claim c8 [qualitative]: The model's estimated value function shows nodes with lower heights (closer to leaf nodes) receive more accurate and definitive probability evaluations, whereas nodes with greater heights exhibit more ambiguous evaluations, reflecting increased uncertainty

GPT-2 Through the Lens of Vector Symbolic Architectures (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: vsa, vector-symbolic-architectures, bundling-binding, nearly-orthogonal-vectors, mechanistic-interpretability, residual-stream, concept-circuits
The paper interprets GPT-2's residual stream as a vector symbolic architecture (VSA) where nearly orthogonal concept vectors are bundled (summed) and bound (matrix-multiplied) across layers. Word embeddings serve as initial concept vectors; attention layers perform unbinding-bundling-rebinding operations to focus on specific token packages and aggregate their similarities; MLP blocks implement boolean functions (OR, AND, NOT) on bundled concept vectors by checking weighted sums against thresholds, with outputs written back to the stream as new nearly orthogonal concepts. Layer normalization and biases support near-zero mean maintenance and auxiliary 'cleaning', while the unembedding projects residual directions back to token probabilities via dot products with word embeddings.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Word embeddings of GPT-2 are nearly orthogonal to each other and can be interpreted as concept vectors.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Attention layers can be interpreted as a sequence of unbinding operations (Q-transformation), bundling of selected vectors based on relevance, and rebinding (output projection), effectively focusing on and repackaging specific concept packages.
claim c3 [quantitative]: More than 80% of neurons in the first MLP layer have weights that can be explained by simple bundled vectors (sums of word embeddings) with a cosine similarity of 0.7 or higher.
claim c4 [quantitative]: At least 95% of neurons in the first MLP layer can be explained by bundled vectors with a cosine similarity of at least 0.5, including attributions to attention heads.
claim c5 [quantitative]: 30–50% of neurons in middle layers can be explained by bundled vectors composed of output projection vectors from attention and MLP blocks with a cosine similarity of at least 0.7, with most bundlings composed of up to 40 vectors.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Attention and MLP output projection matrices are composed of nearly orthogonal vectors, as evidenced by strong diagonals with minimal off-diagonal artifacts in M^T M products.
claim c7 [qualitative]: The mean of word embeddings and intermediate hidden embeddings across multiple prompts is near zero, suggesting low angular distortion of concept directions across layers despite layer normalization.
claim c8 [negative]: Experiments are limited to GPT-2 small; further experiments are needed to confirm that these bundling and binding behaviors also exist in related and more recent models.

Concept Bottleneck Large Language Models (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: concept-bottleneck, neuron-steering, concept-detection, adversarial-disentanglement, interpretable-generation, activation-manipulation
CB-LLM transforms pretrained LLMs into interpretable models by inserting a Concept Bottleneck Layer (CBL) between the backbone's hidden representations and task-specific heads. For classification, text embeddings pass through CBL (trained to align with concept scores), ReLU, then a sparse linear layer for class logits. For generation, CBL and an unsupervised layer are concatenated and fed to an unembedding head; adversarial training forces the unsupervised layer to remove concept information, preserving CBL's steerability. Concept scores are derived from sentence embeddings (all-mpnet-base-v2) and refined by masking misaligned class–concept pairs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CB-LLM w/ ACC achieves test accuracy competitive with or matching finetuned black-box RoBERTa-base across SST2, YelpP, AGnews, and DBpedia
claim c2 [quantitative]: Automatic Concept Scoring (ACS) takes 1.6 hours on YelpP and DBpedia datasets (largest), versus 8.8 hours for LLM-based labeling on just 1,000 samples per dataset
claim c3 [qualitative]: CB-LLM w/ ACC achieves higher average human rating on Activation Faithfulness task compared to TBMC3M baseline
claim c4 [qualitative]: CB-LLM (generation) achieves concept detection accuracy within ~3% of Llama3-8B finetuned for direct concept classification
claim c5 [qualitative]: CB-LLM (generation) achieves significantly higher steerability score with adversarial training (Module 2) versus without, where steerability without adversarial training is close to random
claim c6 [qualitative]: CB-LLM (generation) achieves perplexity comparable to standard finetuned Llama3-8B, indicating no degradation in generation quality
claim c7 [quantitative]: Toxicity detection chatbot achieves 92% accuracy on toxicity detection and 91% steerability score on test set
claim c8 [qualitative]: CB-LLM enables concept unlearning, allowing users to deactivate neurons in CBL to remove biased or subjective concepts; case study shows 2,726 out of 10k test samples shifted from negative to positive after unlearning 'overpriced' concept

Large Concept Models: Language Modeling in a Sentence Representation Space (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sentence-embeddings, concept-level-reasoning, diffusion-models, residual-quantization, multilingual, multimodal, frozen-encoder-decoder
Large Concept Models (LCM) operate on sentence-level embeddings from the SONAR space rather than tokens. The input is segmented into sentences, each encoded to a fixed-size vector using frozen SONAR encoders. A transformer-based model (decoder-only) then processes sequences of these concept embeddings autoregressively to predict the next concept. The generated concept embeddings are decoded back into text (or speech) using frozen SONAR decoders, enabling language- and modality-agnostic reasoning. Multiple architectures are explored: Base-LCM (MSE regression), One-Tower Diffusion LCM (single transformer predicting clean embeddings from noisy input), Two-Tower Diffusion LCM (separate contextualizer and denoiser with cross-attention), and Quantized LCM (residual vector quantization of SONAR space followed by discrete or continuous prediction).
claim c1 [qualitative]: Models trained using 1.6B parameter models and training data in the order of 1.3T tokens
claim c2 [qualitative]: One architecture scaled to a model size of 7B parameters and training data of about 2.7T tokens
claim c3 [qualitative]: LCM exhibits impressive zero-shot generalization performance to many languages, outperforming existing LLMs of the same size
claim c4 [qualitative]: SONAR embedding space supports 200 languages for text input and output, 76 languages for speech input, and speech output in English
claim c5 [qualitative]: SaT Capped sentence segmentation method with 200 character capping achieves higher AutoBLEU scores than SpaCy Capped across all sentence lengths
claim c6 [quantitative]: Residual Vector Quantization with all 64 codebooks reaches around 70% of the auto-encoding BLEU score achieved with continuous SONAR embeddings
claim c7 [qualitative]: LCM operates on sequences which are at least an order of magnitude shorter than token sequences, assuming an average sentence length of 10–20 tokens
claim c8 [qualitative]: The model can be applied to any language and modality supported by SONAR encoders in zero-shot without additional data or fine-tuning

Constrained Decoding with Speculative Lookaheads (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: speculative-decoding, constrained-decoding, lookahead-heuristic, draft-verify, reward-scoring, state-machine
CDSL combines constrained decoding with speculative decoding. A small draft LLM autoregressively generates speculative lookahead tokens. These tokens are validated by a larger target LLM via forward pass (hard rejection or speculative sampling) and scored by a task-specific reward function. Based on acceptance and reward scores, the system decides via state-based rules whether to accept drafted tokens, sample from the target LLM with constraint guidance, or fall back to CDLH-approximate beam search. The process repeats until target sequence length is reached.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CDSL achieves 2.2x to 12.15x speedup over CDLH without significant performance reduction
claim c2 [qualitative]: On CommonGen, CDSL achieves hard constraint satisfaction gain compared to greedy decoding
claim c3 [qualitative]: On Harmless Text Generation, CDSL achieves gain compared to greedy decoding
claim c4 [qualitative]: CDLH-Appx. baseline outperforms greedy by in CommonGen and in HTG tasks
claim c5 [qualitative]: Hard rejection achieves slightly better speedup compared to speculative sampling, but hard rejection consistently achieves higher performance
claim c6 [qualitative]: Nucleus sampling does not improve constraint satisfaction performance; increased diversity does not contribute to better adherence to constraints
claim c7 [negative]: CDSL shows a runtime-performance tradeoff compared to the performance skyline CDLH; however, the performance tradeoff is not drastic compared to the runtime skyline greedy decoding
claim c8 [negative]: Approach requires target and draft models from the same LLM family due to tokenizer mismatch; this is a general limitation of speculative decoding-based approaches

Entropy-Regularized Process Reward Model (2024)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, entropy-regularization, kl-regularized-mdp, best-of-n-decoding, rejection-sampling-rlhf, multi-step-reasoning
ER-PRM is a process reward model for multi-step LLM reasoning that scores individual intermediate steps rather than final outcomes. It derives reward values from KL-regularized MDPs, computing step rewards using the reference (initial) policy's completions via a soft-max formulation. The model is trained as an auto-regressive or regression-based classifier on step-level supervision, then applied at inference time for best-of-N selection or during RLHF rejection sampling to guide policy optimization.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ER-PRM achieves 1% improvement on GSM8K and 2-3% improvement on MATH under best-of-N evaluation
claim c2 [quantitative]: ER-PRM achieves more than 1% improvement under RLHF
claim c3 [qualitative]: Entropy-regularized process reward model can be computed using the reference policy to generate completions, eliminating the need to learn reward and policy simultaneously
claim c4 [quantitative]: For Mistral-MetaMath-7B under RLHF rejection sampling, ER-PRM surpasses Soft-Label PRM by 0.8% on GSM8K and 2.7% on MATH
claim c5 [quantitative]: For DeepSeek-math-7B-instruct under RLHF rejection sampling, ER-PRM achieves 83.4±0.3% on GSM8K and 44.7±0.7% on MATH
claim c6 [quantitative]: 3B reward model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) on Mistral data achieves 41.9±1.0% best-of-64 accuracy on MATH500, approaching performance of 8B model
claim c7 [negative]: Regression-based training yields consistently lower best-of-N accuracies than auto-regressive training, with a significant gap on MATH
claim c8 [negative]: 1B reward model (Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct) suffers from severe reward hacking on MATH, with accuracy dropping ~2% from best-of-16 to best-of-64

Efficient Knowledge Injection in LLMs via Self-Distillation (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: self-distillation, logit-matching, knowledge-injection, lora, kl-divergence, soft-targets
Prompt distillation is a self-distillation method for knowledge injection into LLMs. A teacher model with access to new knowledge K in its prompt generates logit distributions over answer tokens for question-answer pairs. A student model (initialized identically to the teacher, with a trainable LoRA adapter) receives only the question and answer, and is trained via KL divergence to match the teacher's logit distributions. The teacher remains frozen; only the student's LoRA adapter is updated. The method requires no external retrieval, no larger teacher models, and no structured knowledge formats—only free-form documents from which question-answer pairs are synthetically generated at high temperature for diversity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Prompt distillation significantly outperforms supervised fine-tuning on Squadshifts closed-book QA and can match retrieval-augmented generation performance
claim c2 [quantitative]: Prompt distillation combined with RAG achieves the highest overall performance across evaluated datasets
claim c3 [qualitative]: Prompt distillation is substantially more data-efficient than supervised fine-tuning; to match PD performance with 20 training questions, SFT requires an order of magnitude more training data
claim c4 [qualitative]: Prompt distillation's higher data efficiency compensates for its additional forward pass, making it more time-efficient than supervised fine-tuning
claim c5 [qualitative]: Self-distillation (where expert = teacher = student model) outperforms using larger models as expert or teacher, because style mismatches between larger and smaller models overshadow benefits of greater model capacity
claim c6 [qualitative]: Using higher sampling temperatures for training data generation and distillation is critical to prompt distillation performance; reducing either temperature degrades results
claim c7 [qualitative]: Prompt distillation requires neither larger teacher models nor structured knowledge formats, enabling efficient internalization of complex factual knowledge from free-form documents
claim c8 [negative]: Supervised fine-tuning does not reach the asymptotic performance of prompt distillation, even with substantially more training data

Reasoning Language Models: A Blueprint (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: mcts, beam-search, rl-reasoning, process-supervision, outcome-supervision, tree-search, policy-value-models, test-time-compute
A comprehensive blueprint organizing Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) into a modular framework combining three pipelines (inference, training, data generation). Inference constructs reasoning structures (chains, trees, graphs, nested forms) by iteratively applying operators (generate, select, aggregate, prune, restructure, refine) guided by reasoning strategies (MCTS, Beam Search, ensemble methods). Policy and value models score candidate steps; training incorporates both supervised (PRM800K) and self-learning phases with outcome-based or process-based supervision. Data generation mirrors inference to enable self-play-like continuous improvement. The framework unifies components through operators (structure, traversal, update) applied to reasoning structures under different reasoning strategies.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RLMs combine LLMs with advanced reasoning mechanisms through integration of reinforcement learning, search heuristics, and structured exploration
claim c2 [qualitative]: The blueprint encompasses diverse reasoning structures (chains, trees, graphs, and nested forms), reasoning strategies (Monte Carlo Tree Search, Beam Search, ensemble methods), and supervision schemes (Outcome-Based and Process-Based Supervision)
claim c3 [qualitative]: Existing schemes such as LLaMA-Berry, QwQ, Journey Learning, and Graph of Thoughts fit as special cases within the unified blueprint
claim c4 [qualitative]: Multi-phase training for policy and value models is key to effective RLM construction
claim c5 [qualitative]: Familiar training distributions (distributions close to the model's pretraining distribution) are important for RLM performance
claim c6 [qualitative]: RLMs enable extrapolation beyond training data boundaries by combining structured exploration with LLM knowledge, in contrast to standard LLMs which primarily perform interpolation within their training distribution
claim c7 [qualitative]: Reasoning steps can vary in granularity from single tokens to entire reasoning chains or logical arguments; coarse-grained steps simplify data preparation and enhance interpretability while reducing computational overhead, whereas fine-grained steps enable incorporation of model uncertainty and advanced decoding schemes
claim c8 [qualitative]: A modular implementation (x1) has been created to simplify development and experimentation with new RLM architectures, supporting training, inference, synthetic data generation, batch processing, and cloud deployment integration

Coarse-to-Fine Process Reward Modeling for Mathematical Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, step-merging, redundancy-mitigation, multi-granularity-training, mathematical-reasoning
CFPRM is a data collection and training strategy for Process Reward Models (PRMs) in mathematical reasoning. It operates on step sequences generated by LLMs: a sliding window of size w initially merges consecutive reasoning steps into coarse-grained steps (labeled by the last step's label), then w is progressively decreased until reaching 1, collecting training data at multiple granularities. All collected data (coarse and fine-grained) are combined and used to train a PRM end-to-end via standard supervised learning objectives (BCE, MSE, or Q-ranking) applied to the LLM backbone. At inference, the trained PRM scores individual steps.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Reasoning steps generated by LLMs often fail to exhibit strictly incremental information, leading to redundancy that can hinder effective reasoning.
claim c2 [qualitative]: CFPRM improves upon ReSTMCTS* by 1.3% on GSM-Plus and 3.4% on MATH500 when employing MSE as the loss function
claim c3 [qualitative]: CFPRM consistently brings performance improvements across all configurations (BoN@8, BoN@16, BoN@32, BoN@64) across three loss criteria (BCE, MSE, Q-ranking) on two datasets
claim c4 [qualitative]: When integrating CFPRM with the Q-ranking-based method (PQM), performance on GSM-Plus averages to 71.3% (BoN) and on MATH500 averages to 84.1% (BoN)
claim c5 [qualitative]: The Q-ranking-based method is more sensitive to window size w; when w is set to 4, performance on GSM-Plus shows no improvement over baseline, and performance on MATH500 lags behind the baseline
claim c6 [qualitative]: Setting w to 2 or 3 can better boost overall Q-ranking performance; the optimal merge window size is not fixed across different loss criteria
claim c7 [negative]: The optimal window size w should be designed adaptively with respect to different loss criteria, which is not addressed in this work
claim c8 [negative]: A more reasonable method should involve detecting and removing redundant steps, which is not discussed in this work

A Survey of Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Customized Large Language Models (2025)
seam: text; tags: survey, graph-rag, knowledge-retrieval, structured-knowledge, rag-augmentation
This is a survey paper on Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG), a paradigm that augments large language models with structured knowledge retrieval. GraphRAG structures domain knowledge as graphs (either as explicit knowledge carriers or indexing tools) and retrieves relevant subgraphs to augment LLM generation. The system flow is: (1) Knowledge Organization—convert unstructured text into graph-structured representations or use graphs to index text chunks; (2) Knowledge Retrieval—use graph-based planners (learnable or algorithm-based) to retrieve query-relevant subgraphs; (3) Knowledge Integration—incorporate retrieved subgraph information into the LLM prompt. GraphRAG addresses traditional RAG limitations in complex query understanding, distributed knowledge integration, context window constraints, and scalability by enabling multi-hop reasoning and pruning irrelevant information through structured graph navigation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Traditional RAG systems based on flat text retrieval face three critical challenges: (i) complex query understanding in professional contexts, (ii) difficulties in knowledge integration across distributed sources, and (iii) system efficiency bottlenecks at scale
claim c2 [qualitative]: GraphRAG addresses traditional RAG limitations through three key innovations: (i) graph-structured knowledge representation that explicitly captures entity relationships and domain hierarchies, (ii) efficient graph-based retrieval techniques that enable context-preserving knowledge retrieval with multihop reasoning ability, and (iii) structure-aware knowledge integration algorithms that leverage retrieved knowledge for accurate and logical coherent generation
claim c3 [qualitative]: Traditional RAG systems that rely on simple keyword matching and vector similarity techniques are inadequate for capturing the deep semantic nuances and multi-step reasoning processes necessary for accurate and comprehensive understanding. When queried about the connection between concept A and concept D, these systems typically retrieve only directly related information, missing crucial intermediate concepts like B and C that could bridge the relationship
claim c4 [qualitative]: Domain knowledge is usually collected from different sources with varying levels of quality, accuracy, and completeness. The retrieved knowledge is often flattened, extensive, and intricate, while domain concepts are typically scattered across multiple documents without clear hierarchical relationships
claim c5 [qualitative]: LLMs are constrained by their fixed context window (typically 2K-32K tokens). Long-range dependencies in complex documents cannot be fully captured, as content exceeding the context window must be truncated or summarized, disrupting natural semantic units and logical flow
claim c6 [qualitative]: Existing GraphRAG models can be categorized into three main categories: (1) Knowledge-based GraphRAG, which uses graphs as knowledge carriers; (2) Index-based GraphRAG which uses graphs as index tools to retrieve relevant raw texts from the corpus; and (3) Hybrid GraphRAG which combines the strengths of both knowledge-based and index-based frameworks
claim c7 [qualitative]: Knowledge-based GraphRAG focuses on transforming unstructured textual documents into explicit and structured knowledge graphs, where nodes represent domain concepts and edges capture semantic relationships. Index-based GraphRAG maintains the original textual form while utilizing graph structures primarily as an indexing mechanism to organize and retrieve relevant text chunks efficiently
claim c8 [qualitative]: GraphRAG utilizes graph structures to represent knowledge, capturing complex relationships between entities and concepts, allowing for a more nuanced and contextual unde

Sparse Autoencoders Trained on the Same Data Learn Different Features (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, feature-discovery, interpretability, alignment-matching, seed-sensitivity
Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) consist of an encoder that transforms LLM activation vectors into a sparse, higher-dimensional latent space, and a decoder that projects latents back to the original space. Both are trained jointly to minimize reconstruction error while maintaining sparsity. The paper measures feature stability by training multiple SAEs on identical data with different random seeds, then applying the Hungarian algorithm to compute optimal bijective matchings of latents between independently trained SAEs based on cosine similarity of encoder and decoder vectors.
claim c1 [quantitative]: In an SAE with 131K latents trained on a feedforward network in Llama 3 8B, only 30% of the features were shared across different seeds
claim c2 [quantitative]: For a 32K latent SAE trained on the sixth MLP of Pythia 160M, only 42% of latents are shared across two independently trained SAEs
claim c3 [quantitative]: When training nine independent SAEs with different seeds, the number of latents found in only one SAE is reduced to about 35%
claim c4 [qualitative]: ReLU SAEs trained with the L1 sparsity loss showed greater stability across seeds compared to SAEs using the state-of-the-art TopK activation function, which were more seed-dependent, even when controlling for the level of sparsity
claim c5 [qualitative]: Increasing the number of SAE latents decreases the overlap between different seeds
claim c6 [qualitative]: Latents that most frequently fire in the base SAE are the ones that are shared with all eight other SAE seeds, while those that infrequently fire are not shared with any other SAE
claim c7 [qualitative]: Orphan features (found in only one SAE seed) are often quite interpretable, with a significant fraction of latents found only on one SAE having high-scoring explanations
claim c8 [negative]: The set of features uncovered by an SAE should be viewed as a pragmatically useful decomposition of activation space, rather than an exhaustive and universal list of features truly used by the model

Low-Rank Adapting Models for Sparse Autoencoders (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: lora-fine-tuning, sae-reconstruction, kl-divergence-alignment, interpretability, post-hoc-adaptation
The system freezes a pre-trained sparse autoencoder (SAE) inserted after a specific transformer layer, then trains low-rank adapters (LoRA) on the frozen language model's weight matrices in all subsequent MLP and attention sublayers. The SAE reconstructs activations from the original model; these reconstructions propagate through the remaining layers. LoRA adapters (low-rank updates to frozen weights) are trained to minimize KL divergence between the next-token logits of the SAE-modified model and the original model, thus learning to compensate for SAE reconstruction error without modifying the SAE or original model weights.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our method reduces the cross entropy loss gap by 30% to 55% when SAEs are inserted during the forward pass.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Compared to end-to-end (e2e) SAEs, our approach achieves the same downstream cross entropy loss 3× to 20× faster on Gemma and 2× to 10× faster on Llama.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Our approach achieves 130 fewer language model backward passes than e2e SAEs on Gemma-2-2B and 40 fewer backward passes on Llama-3.2-1B.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Training LoRA adapters on just the first layer after the SAE achieves 88.14% of the loss reduction achieved by training LoRA adapters on all layers after the SAE.
claim c5 [quantitative]: For multiple SAEs on Llama-3.1-8B: compound cross entropy loss for 7 SAEs reduced from 7.83 nats to 2.78 nats; for 3 SAEs from 3.55 nats to 2.45 nats.
claim c6 [quantitative]: On SAEBench metrics for Gemma-2-2B TopK SAE: Targeted Probe Perturbation (TPP) max improved from 0.372 to 0.412; Spurious Correlation Removal (SCR) max from 0.448 to 0.526; Sparse Probing (top 1) from 0.732 to 0.760.
claim c7 [qualitative]: The LoRA-adapted model improves at activation steering by scaling SAE latents; for the Donald Trump latent, the LoRA model shows increased likelihood on positive examples while minimizing increases on negative examples.
claim c8 [negative]: End-to-end SAEs achieve a lower final cross entropy loss than our method on Llama-3.2-1B, though not on Gemma-2-2B.

Scaling Flaws of Verifier-Guided Search in Mathematical Reasoning (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: beam-search, verifier-scoring, candidate-ranking, inference-time-scaling, mathematical-reasoning
The paper evaluates verifier-guided search (beam search with step-level candidate ranking) for mathematical reasoning in LLMs. A generator produces candidate partial solution paths at each step; a verifier (OVM or PRM) scores candidates; top-k paths advance to the next step. The system compares this against repeated sampling baselines. The paper is empirical/analytical: it measures coverage (fraction of problems with ≥1 correct solution) as sample size and candidate count scale, identifies root causes of performance degradation, and tests two inference-time modifications (stochastic selection, Monte Carlo rollout) to reduce verifier reliance.
claim c1 [qualitative]: PRM-guided search based on DeepSeekMath achieves over 20% higher performance than repeated sampling when sample size is 1, but by sample size of 16, verifier-guided search becomes inferior to repeated sampling, reaching approximately 5% lower performance when scaled to 32
claim c2 [qualitative]: OVM-guided search performance degradation on GSM8K is approximately 10%, increasing to around 20% on MATH (measured at sample size 32 vs. sample size 1)
claim c3 [qualitative]: Scaling flaws are more severe on OOD problems: OVM-guided search performance degradation increases from 19.3% on in-distribution Level 4 to 25.8% in OOD-L4 setting at sample size 32
claim c4 [qualitative]: A large proportion of OVM-guided search failures occur during the selection stage: 88.6% on GSM8K (Mistral), 80.0% on MATH (DeepSeekMath), 84.3% on OOD-L4, and 81.2% on OOD-L5
claim c5 [quantitative]: Stochastic selection with temperature 1 improves selection stage accuracy over OVM deterministic selection by up to 11.2% on OOD-L4 and OOD-L5
claim c6 [qualitative]: One-time Monte Carlo rollout with λ=1 (full reliance on simulated reward) yields the highest accuracy gain, underscoring that relying entirely on rollout reward without verifier-predicted score outperforms verifier evaluation
claim c7 [negative]: Increasing the number of generated candidates fails to improve and even degrades the performance of verifier-guided search
claim c8 [negative]: Verifier-guided search is designed to improve performance on difficult problems, yet these challenging scenarios are more susceptible to verifier failures, exacerbating scaling flaws and contradicting the intended purpose

Improving Rule-based Reasoning in LLMs using Neurosymbolic Representations (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, vsa, symbolic-algorithms, hidden-state-intervention, mathematical-reasoning, neurosymbolic-bridge
A neurosymbolic method that intercepts LLM hidden states at layer 17, encodes them into fixed-dimensional HRR (Holographic Reduced Representation) vectors via a trained linear encoder, applies rule-based symbolic algorithms (modulo, GCD, LCM, bitwise ops, etc.) within the VSA space by querying digit and operation components, decodes the solution vector back to hidden-state space via a trained linear decoder, and merges the decoded result (50-50 weighted sum) with the original hidden state before passing to downstream layers. Inference gates the intervention via dot-product similarity threshold (0.8) to skip unknown problem types.
claim c1 [quantitative]: average of 88.6% lower cross-entropy loss and 15.4 times more problems correctly solved on a suite of mathematical reasoning tasks compared to chain-of-thought prompting and supervised fine-tuning (LoRA)
claim c2 [quantitative]: On modulo tasks, NS LLM achieves 98.7% accuracy with 0.093 loss, compared to Standard LLM 53.5% / 2.904 loss, CoT 69.7% / 4.424 loss, LoRA 51.5% / 3.838 loss
claim c3 [quantitative]: On multiplication tasks, NS LLM achieves 95.6% accuracy versus Standard LLM 1.1%, CoT 25.3%, LoRA 4.5%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Encoder achieves minimum RMSE loss at layer 17 of LLaMA 3.1 8B; encoding error rate for individual digits is under 2% at this layer
claim c5 [qualitative]: Out-of-distribution tasks (Addition and Integer Division, unseen during training) show NS LLM achieving 98.2% and 97.4% accuracy respectively, compared to LoRA's 46.5% and 72.0%, demonstrating generalization by gating interventions on problem-type similarity
claim c6 [qualitative]: Computational overhead of neurosymbolic processing per forward pass is O(D²) in VSA dimension D, dominated by standard KV-cached transformer cost of O(n²) (sequence length n), and space overhead is O(D²), negligible relative to full LLM memory
claim c7 [negative]: On more complex tasks such as LCM (87.3% score) and square modulo (58.9% score), NS LLM performance is lower, likely due to encoding error accumulation and the complexity of forward-pass algorithms requiring multi-hop reasoning that a single decoder intervention cannot fully capture
claim c8 [qualitative]: CoT baseline exhibits higher loss (0.874–16.606 across tasks) and inconsistent improvements; on bitwise XOR CoT degrades from 6.7% (Standard) to 1.1%, attributed to error accumulation in multi-step intermediate reasoning steps such as incorrect bitstring conversion

Token Assorted: Mixing Latent and Text Tokens for Improved Language Model Reasoning (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vq-vae-compression, hybrid-token-mixing, cot-abstraction, discrete-latent, vocabulary-extension, randomized-replacement
A two-stage hybrid reasoning system that compresses chain-of-thought (CoT) traces using discrete latent tokens. Stage 1: A VQ-VAE (vector-quantized variational autoencoder) with a 50M-parameter encoder-decoder pair learns to map text token sequences into discrete latent codes organized in a fixed codebook. Stage 2: An LLM is fine-tuned on modified training data where the leftmost N CoT tokens are replaced with their latent abstractions (demarcated by special tokens), while later tokens remain as raw text. During training, N is randomly sampled per example to expose the model to varying degrees of latent-text mixing; at inference, the LLM generates latent tokens directly without invoking the VQ-VAE. The approach treats latent tokens as vocabulary extensions, requiring no external module at test time.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On Math benchmark with Llama-3.2-1B, latent approach achieves +4.2 percentage points over the second-best baseline (CoT).
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K benchmark with Llama-3.2-3B, latent approach achieves +4.1 percentage points over CoT.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Fresh-Gaokao-Math-2023 benchmark with Llama-3.1-8B, latent approach achieves +13.3 percentage points over CoT.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Across all mathematical reasoning benchmarks tested (Math, GSM8K, Gaokao-Math-2023, DeepMind-Math, College-Math, OlympiaBench-Math, TheoremQA), latent approach achieves an average of 17% reduction in reasoning trace length compared to CoT while improving accuracy.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Keys-Finding Maze synthetic benchmark with T5 trained from scratch, latent approach achieves 62.8% on 1-Feasible-10 metric, a +19.8 percentage point improvement over CoT (43%).
claim c6 [quantitative]: On ProsQA logical reasoning benchmark with GPT-2 trained from scratch, latent approach achieves 96.2% accuracy, a +18.7 percentage point improvement over CoT (77.5%).
claim c7 [qualitative]: Randomized replacement strategy (varying the number of text tokens replaced per sample) consistently outperforms curriculum learning and all-replace strategies, enabling fast single-stage adaptation to unseen latent tokens without multi-epoch curriculum training.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Attention weight analysis shows that models trained with latent-text mixing focus more attention on numerically significant tokens and tokens representing mathematical operations (e.g., 'earns', 'cost'), compared to baseline CoT models, suggesting improved capture of essential reasoning information.

Universal Sparse Autoencoders: Interpretable Cross-Model Concept Alignment (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, multi-model-alignment, concept-discovery, activation-reconstruction, coordinated-activation-maximization
Universal Sparse Autoencoders (USAEs) learn a shared, overcomplete sparse dictionary that encodes activations from multiple pretrained vision models into a unified concept space and decodes them back to reconstruct each model's activations. During training, a single model is randomly selected per iteration; its activations are encoded into the shared sparse code via a model-specific encoder, then decoded via all model-specific decoders to reconstruct every model's activations. Backpropagation updates only the chosen encoder-decoder pair against the aggregated reconstruction loss across all models, enforcing concept alignment during end-to-end training.
claim c1 [qualitative]: USAEs discover semantically coherent universal concepts across vision models ranging from low-level features (e.g., colors and textures) to higher-level structures (e.g., parts and objects)
claim c2 [quantitative]: Self-reconstruction R² scores for DinoV2 average 0.8, compared to 0.7 for SigLIP and ViT on final layer activations
claim c3 [quantitative]: Over 1000 concepts exhibit peak firing entropy, confirming the USAE learns a strongly universal concept space; bimodal distribution shows a second group with moderate entropy indicating concepts that favor two models but not all three
claim c4 [quantitative]: The first 200 concepts co-fire between 50–80% of the time across all three models, indicating a core set of consistently recognized concepts
claim c5 [quantitative]: When focusing on high-frequency co-firing concepts, the correlation between concept co-firing frequency and concept energy strengthens from r=0.35 (all concepts) to r=0.66 (frequently co-firing concepts), suggesting a strong correlation between how energetic a concept is and its universality
claim c6 [quantitative]: ViT has the strongest concept overlap with 41% of its universal concepts having a cosine similarity ≥0.5 with its independent SAE counterpart; SigLIP and DinoV2 achieve 30% and 36% respectively; USAEs achieve AUC=0.30 versus baseline AUC=0.13 on concept consistency matching
claim c7 [qualitative]: DinoV2 exhibits unique geometric and perspective-related concepts including converging perspective lines, view-invariant angle/tilt features, and low-level 3D understanding concepts such as rectangular prism faces and brim lines of dome-shaped objects, which are absent or low-entropy in SigLIP and ViT
claim c8 [negative]: Only a relatively low proportion of concepts show high overlap between universal and independently trained SAEs (30% for SigLIP, 36% for DinoV2 at cosine similarity ≥0.5), indicating universal training discovers concepts that may not emerge in independent training

Sparse Autoencoders Do Not Find Canonical Units of Analysis (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-analysis, feature-decomposition, activation-steering, interpretability-probe, dictionary-learning
The paper introduces two analysis techniques for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on LLM activations. SAE stitching swaps latent directions between SAEs of different dictionary sizes, measuring reconstruction performance changes to classify latents as novel (capturing new information) or reconstruction (refining existing features). Meta-SAEs are SAEs trained on the decoder directions of a larger SAE to decompose those directions into interpretable sparse combinations of meta-latents, revealing compositional structure. Information flows through decoder direction similarity metrics (cosine similarity thresholds) and reconstruction loss measurements.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Smaller SAEs are incomplete: larger SAEs learn novel latents that improve performance when added, indicating they capture information missed by smaller SAEs
claim c2 [qualitative]: Larger SAE latents are not atomic but often decompose into combinations of latents from smaller SAEs due to the sparsity penalty incentivizing composition of features
claim c3 [quantitative]: Meta-SAE trained on GPT-2 SAE decoder directions (dictionary size 49152) explains 55.47% of variance of the decoder directions
claim c4 [quantitative]: Meta-SAE latent explanations evaluated in zero-shot multiple-choice: GPT-4o-mini chose the correct SAE latent explanation 73% of the time among 5 options on 1,000 sampled latents
claim c5 [qualitative]: Example latent decompositions show interpretability: a latent representing 'Einstein' decomposes into meta-latents for 'scientist', 'Germany', 'famous person', and other related concepts
claim c6 [negative]: SAEs do not find a canonical set of units: rather than converging on a unique, complete, and irreducible set of features, SAEs of different sizes offer varying granularities and compositions of features
claim c7 [negative]: The paper acknowledges its work only studies two LLMs (GPT-2 Small and Gemma 2 2B) and does not include very large SAEs such as in Templeton (2024)
claim c8 [qualitative]: Meta-SAE latents show high maximum cosine similarity to latents from SAEs of comparable size trained directly on network activations, supporting the hypothesis that larger SAE latents are compositions of features from smaller SAEs

Flexible and Efficient Grammar-Constrained Decoding (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, grammar-enforcement, token-masking, lexer-alignment, context-free-grammar
GreatGramma is a grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) system that prevents LLMs from generating outputs violating a context-free grammar (CFG). It operates in two phases: (1) offline preprocessing constructs a token-spanner table by analyzing how the LLM's subword tokenizer aligns with CFG terminals defined as regular expressions, building a lexer FSA and mapping LLM tokens to sequences of terminals; (2) online decoding uses this table to compute boolean masks over the LLM vocabulary at each step, masking tokens that provably cannot lead to valid parses. The core innovation addresses token misalignment—LLM tokens may span multiple, partial, or no CFG terminals—by precomputing a lexer-state-dependent mapping.
claim c1 [qualitative]: GreatGramma achieves an average 17.71x speedup in offline preprocessing compared to related approaches
claim c2 [qualitative]: GreatGramma maintains state-of-the-art online masking efficiency of 5-32ms per token
claim c3 [qualitative]: Existing GCD algorithms require tens of minutes to preprocess common grammars
claim c4 [negative]: GCD implementations from existing published papers contain soundness bugs (e.g., they mask tokens that should not be masked or do not terminate when preprocessing simple grammars)
claim c5 [qualitative]: The key challenge with implementing GCD algorithms is that the tokens used by subword tokenizers in LLMs do not align with the terminals used by parsers
claim c6 [qualitative]: Grammar-constrained decoding can guarantee that LLM outputs match specified context-free grammar rules by masking out tokens that will provably lead to outputs not in the grammar

Scaling up Test-Time Compute with Latent Reasoning: A Recurrent Depth Approach (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: recurrent-depth, latent-reasoning, test-time-compute-scaling, iterative-refinement, adaptive-compute, truncated-bptt
A decoder-only transformer with latent depth recurrence: input tokens are embedded by a prelude block into latent space, then a shared recurrent core block iterates on the hidden state (injecting the embedded input at each step), unrolling to arbitrary depth at test-time. A coda block un-embeds the final latent state to produce next-token logits. The recurrent block is trained with randomly sampled iteration counts from a log-normal Poisson distribution; inference backpropagates through only the last iterations (truncated BPTT in depth). Architecture uses sandwich normalization (norm-layer-norm), RoPE attention, gated SiLU MLPs, and an adapter that concatenates initial embedding with current state.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The model can improve its performance on reasoning benchmarks up to a computation load equivalent to 50 billion parameters
claim c2 [quantitative]: GSM8K CoT performance with flexible match reaches 38.13% at depth 32
claim c3 [quantitative]: On HumanEval pass@1, the model achieves 23.17% with recurrence depth 32
claim c4 [quantitative]: Recurrent model at 180B tokens achieves 9.02/10.24 (flexible/strict) on GSM8K CoT, versus fixed-depth baseline at 1.82/2.20
claim c5 [qualitative]: Does not require specialized training data; can work with small context windows; can capture types of reasoning not easily represented in words
claim c6 [qualitative]: Trained for 800 billion tokens at 3.5 billion parameters on Frontier supercomputer with 4096 GPUs
claim c7 [negative]: On standard benchmarks, the model lags behind later OLMo models trained on larger, more carefully curated datasets; lags behind dedicated code models like StarCoder2
claim c8 [qualitative]: Recurrent depth models naturally support per-token adaptive compute, self-speculative decoding, and KV-cache sharing at inference time without specialized training or tuning

VersaPRM: Multi-Domain Process Reward Model via Synthetic Reasoning Data (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, step-level-scoring, synthetic-data-generation, multi-domain-reasoning, inference-time-compute, llm-based-labeling
VersaPRM is a Process Reward Model (PRM) that scores the correctness of intermediate reasoning steps in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) sequences. It takes a sequence of reasoning steps as input and outputs a vector of reward scores, one per step. The model is initialized from a math-domain PRM (LlamaPRM800K based on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and fine-tuned via LoRA on synthetic multi-domain reasoning data. At inference, aggregated step scores guide reranking methods (Majority Voting, Weighted Majority Voting, Best-of-N) and search algorithms (Beam Search, MCTS) to select high-quality solutions from multiple candidate CoTs.
claim c1 [negative]: current PRMs have poor performance in other domains. Math PRMs trained exclusively on mathematical data lack the versatility to generalize beyond mathematical reasoning tasks.
claim c2 [quantitative]: in the MMLU-Pro category of Law, VersaPRM via weighted majority voting achieves a 7.9% performance gain over the majority voting baseline
claim c3 [quantitative]: Qwen2.5-Math-PRM's gain in the MMLU-Pro category of Law is 1.3%
claim c4 [qualitative]: For CoTs labeled as correct by the auto-labeler, agreement rate with manual evaluations was 80% (95% CI: 0.69–0.89); for CoTs labeled as incorrect, agreement rate was 71% (95% CI: 0.63–0.79)
claim c5 [qualitative]: VersaPRM consistently outperforms open-source math PRMs in both WMV and BoN across all domain groups (Math, Math-adjacent, and non-Math-adjacent domains)
claim c6 [qualitative]: VersaPRM trained with one domain held out (Law or Biology) remains comparable to the fully trained model across both evaluated domains and surpasses the performance of math PRMs, demonstrating genuine domain-general reasoning capabilities
claim c7 [qualitative]: VersaPRM (random subset) achieves higher WMV accuracy across both math and non-math tasks compared to VersaPRM (Math subset), demonstrating the value of training on diverse data
claim c8 [quantitative]: The CoT generation and labeling pipeline for MMLU-Pro-CoT-Train (Labeled) was completed at a total cost under $100 USD using AWS Bedrock batch inference

Interpreting and Steering Protein Language Models through Sparse Autoencoders (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, mechanistic-interpretability, protein-lm, activation-steering, latent-feature-attribution, sequence-design
The system trains a sparse autoencoder (SAE) on hidden activations from ESM-2 8M (a transformer-based protein language model). The SAE has an overcomplete bottleneck (10× expansion factor) with L1 sparsity regularization to disentangle polysemantic features. Latent components are interpreted via statistical association with protein annotations (precision/recall against UniProt labels). Selected latents are then used to steer sequence generation by scaling/shifting their activations in the residual stream during iterative inference, with reconstructed embeddings injected back before the decoder.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Sparse autoencoders identify latent components associated with various protein characteristics, including transmembrane regions, binding sites, and specialized motifs such as zinc finger domains
claim c2 [quantitative]: 395 putative latent-feature associations are identified with minimum precision or recall of 0.8 at activation threshold 1.00
claim c3 [quantitative]: Intervening on the two most prominent latents (highest recall) associated with zinc finger regions produces 24 matching zinc finger motifs out of 180 generated sequences
claim c4 [quantitative]: Intervening on a single zinc finger latent produces only 3 matches out of 180 sequences, whereas random latent intervention or random latent pair intervention produces zero matches
claim c5 [quantitative]: The selected sparse autoencoder achieves an average of 18 non-zero latent components per amino acid token with a cross-entropy increase of 0.10
claim c6 [qualitative]: Layer selection is guided by intrinsic dimension analysis, identifying layer 3 of ESM-2 8M as a plateau region where abstract information is most clearly encoded
claim c7 [qualitative]: Among 24 matched zinc finger sequences, the highest percent sequence similarity was unreported, with an average sequence similarity of unreported, indicating a good level of diversity among generated sequences
claim c8 [negative]: The sequence generation pipeline requires parameter fine-tuning to improve the success rate; the process is not yet fully automated

Sparse Autoencoder Features for Classifications and Transferability (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-features, activation-extraction, pooling-binarization, interpretability, classification-probe, cross-lingual-transfer
A classification pipeline that extracts pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) features from frozen Gemma 2 LLM hidden states, applies pooling and binarization transformations, and trains logistic regression classifiers on the resulting feature vectors. SAE activations from selected layers are uprojected to sparse dimensions, aggregated via summation or top-k selection, optionally thresholded to binary form, then fed to downstream task-specific classifiers.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE-derived features achieve macro F1 > 0.8, outperforming hidden-state and BoW baselines
claim c2 [quantitative]: Larger models (9B, 9B-IT) achieve consistently higher mean performance (above 0.85 F1) compared to the 2B model
claim c3 [qualitative]: Middle-layer SAE features typically produce higher F1 scores than early- or late-layer features
claim c4 [qualitative]: Binarized and no max pooling of SAE features outperform both hidden-state probes and bag-of-words
claim c5 [quantitative]: SAE features achieve superior F1 scores (0.85-0.90) compared to both hidden states (0.80-0.85) and top-n selection (0.75-0.80) under varying training data sampling rates
claim c6 [quantitative]: Cross-lingual transfer from English achieves 15-20% F1 score decrease compared to native training
