claim c8 [qualitative]: Training objectives can explicitly promote VSA-like behavior through orthogonality constraints on role vectors, auxiliary reconstruction tasks for unbinding, and logic-oriented auxiliary tasks such as permuted-variable reasoning and synthetic theorem-proving to encourage systematic generalization.

JEPA-Reasoner: Decoupling Latent Reasoning from Token Generation (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, token-generation-decoupling, jepa-adaptation, normalized-hypersphere, error-containment, ema-target-encoder, uncertainty-representation
JEPA-Reasoner decouples latent-space reasoning from token generation. The Reasoner component (embedding layer + modified Transformer blocks with hybrid normalization) generates a complete reasoning chain autoregressively within a normalized latent space using scaled cosine similarity loss and EMA target encoder weights. A separate Talker module (Mono-Talker or Dual-Talker variants) then reconstructs tokens from the frozen reasoning trajectory. Information flows: input tokens → Reasoner latent chain (looped back within unit hypersphere) → Talker → output tokens. Token errors cannot backpropagate into reasoning because the Reasoner conditions only on latent states, not on sampled tokens.
claim c1 [quantitative]: JEPA-Reasoner achieves 149.5% improvement in 8-shot GSM8K accuracy over a coupled Transformer baseline trained on identical data
claim c2 [quantitative]: JEPA-Reasoner 8-shot GSM8K accuracy is 51.9% versus Llama 3.2-1B (CoT) 44.4%
claim c3 [quantitative]: JEPA-Reasoner improves 5-shot to 8-shot GSM8K performance by 131.7% (from 39.4% to 51.9%), whereas Transformer baseline stagnates at 0.1% improvement
claim c4 [qualitative]: On CFG token-level error robustness test, JEPA-Reasoner maintains higher accuracy than Transformer baseline under 0–30% token corruption across 4 generation steps
claim c5 [quantitative]: Tree-search task (binary tree depth 4): JEPA-Reasoner + Mono-Talker achieves 99.87% exact match accuracy, with predicted latent vectors forming a continuous cloud within the space spanned by discrete vocabulary embeddings, and sibling-node planes exhibiting average top ranking of 1.72% distance to predicted vectors
claim c6 [qualitative]: JEPA-Reasoner generates mixed latent vectors representing multiple possible reasoning paths simultaneously; for 99.89% of cases, the latent vector of the node on the correct route contributes more than the sibling node in linear combinations
claim c7 [theoretical]: By decoupled architecture design, the sensitivity of future reasoning state with respect to current generated token satisfies ∂z_t'/∂x_t = 0, proving no mathematical pathway for token-space errors to propagate into latent reasoning trajectory
claim c8 [theoretical]: L2 normalization constrains all latent states to the unit hypersphere, bounding Euclidean distance between predicted and optimal reasoning states by the hypersphere diameter; scaled cosine distance loss implicitly minimizes angular divergence from optimal trajectory

ATLAS: Verifier-Guided Adaptive Latent Activation Steering for Efficient LLM Reasoning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, latent-verification, adaptive-action-selection, thought-boundary-intervention, process-reward-distillation
ATLAS is a test-time adaptive steering framework for LLMs that combines latent process verification with activation steering. Offline, it extracts contrastive steering vectors for execution, reflection, and transition modes by comparing hidden states at thought boundaries, and trains a lightweight MLP verifier on PRM-supervised step-quality scores. During inference, at each thought boundary, the verifier scores candidate intervened hidden states induced by four discrete actions (no intervention, execution, reflection, transition), and selects the action with highest predicted quality before generation continues. Information flows from hidden states → verifier → action selection → intervention application → next generation step.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On R1-Distill-1.5B, ATLAS improves MATH accuracy from 73.76% to 82.28% while reducing token usage by 30.1%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On R1-Distill-1.5B, ATLAS improves GSM8K accuracy from 79.30% to 85.37% while reducing token usage by 44.9%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On R1-Distill-1.5B in cross-domain transfer, ATLAS improves AIME2024 accuracy from 20.00% to 33.33% while reducing token usage by 14.8%
claim c4 [quantitative]: On R1-Distill-1.5B in cross-domain transfer, ATLAS improves AMC2023 accuracy from 47.50% to 65.00% with a 26.8% token reduction
claim c5 [quantitative]: ATLAS latent verifier achieves comparable accuracy to text-level PRM verification (ATLAS-T) on in-domain benchmarks while using fewer generated tokens, e.g., 83.83% accuracy with 2035 tokens vs ATLAS-T 83.75% accuracy with 2118 tokens on R1-Distill-1.5B
claim c6 [qualitative]: Smaller models (R1-Distill-1.5B/7B) select reflection and transition actions more frequently, while larger models rely more heavily on execution, suggesting that weaker models benefit more from corrective and exploratory interventions
claim c7 [qualitative]: Latent verifier prediction remains robust under Gaussian perturbations, with absolute prediction change staying insignificant (0.01) for noise scales up to 0.25σ
claim c8 [qualitative]: ATLAS provides a unified framework for combining learned latent verification with test-time activation steering, enabling adaptive reasoning control without additional LLM decoding or inference-time process reward model calls

XGrammar-2: Efficient Dynamic Structured Generation Engine for Agentic LLMs (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, structured-generation, earley-parser, token-mask-cache, tag-dispatch, cross-grammar-reuse, jit-compilation
XGrammar-2 is a structured generation engine for LLM agents that constrains token sampling to valid grammar states via logit masking. It processes grammars in two phases: preprocessing (Earley-based adaptive token mask cache with JIT compilation amortized over decoding steps) and runtime (per-token mask lookup). The system introduces TagDispatch for intra-request tag-triggered switching between free-form text and structured sub-grammars (using Aho-Corasick automaton for tag matching), and Cross-Grammar Cache for reuse of token masks across different grammars via hierarchical FSM hashing. Repetition state compression bounds grammar size for repetitive patterns. Information flows from grammar → Earley parser → scannable state cache keys → token mask lookup → logit zeroing.
claim c1 [quantitative]: XGrammar-2 achieves over 6× faster compilation than prior structured generation engines
claim c2 [quantitative]: XGrammar-2 incurs near-zero end-to-end overhead in modern LLM serving systems
claim c3 [quantitative]: In dynamic tool-calling workloads with 500 tools, substructure reuse rate reaches 95.6% while full-grammar reuse drops to 0%
claim c4 [quantitative]: XGrammar-2 achieves 7× speedup in end-to-end function calling latency compared to XGrammar
claim c5 [quantitative]: Repetition state compression reduces preprocessing time on JSONSchemaBench by 99.6× over baseline Earley parser
claim c6 [quantitative]: XGrammar-2 achieves per-token overhead of approximately 10 ms compilation time versus XGrammar's >1000 ms for dynamic structured generation
claim c7 [qualitative]: TagDispatch introduces a first-class grammar construct for expressing tag-triggered switching between free-form text and structured sub-grammars within a single request, addressing intra-request dynamism more efficiently than plain EBNF encoding
claim c8 [negative]: XGrammar-2 with SGLang produces empty outputs for Qwen3-0.6B and language drift (from English to other languages) for Llama3.1-8B when using llguidance-v1.2.0 baseline for dynamic structured generation

Beyond Outcome Verification: Verifiable Process Reward Models for Structured Reasoning (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: verifiable-rewards, process-supervision, rule-based-verifier, structured-reasoning, reinforcement-learning, step-level-feedback
Verifiable Process Reward Models (VPRMs) integrate deterministic, rule-based verifiers into reinforcement learning to evaluate intermediate reasoning steps in structured tasks. The model generates a reasoning trajectory with step identifiers and step labels; each step is checked against domain guidelines by a deterministic verifier (not a neural judge), yielding step-level rewards. These step rewards are combined with terminal outcome rewards and fed into policy optimization algorithms (GRPO or DAPO) to guide the LLM toward reasoning traces that are both factually correct and logically coherent with task rules.
claim c1 [quantitative]: VPRMs achieve up to 20% higher F1 than state-of-the-art models
claim c2 [quantitative]: VPRMs achieve 6.5% higher F1 than verifiable outcome rewards
claim c3 [quantitative]: On CochraneForest, Qwen2.5-7B-GRPO-VPRM achieves 87.9% Accuracy and 76.7% macro-F1
claim c4 [quantitative]: VPRM-trained models achieve 89.5% Coherence on CochraneForest (proportion of datapoints where final risk prediction is consistent with intermediate reasoning)
claim c5 [quantitative]: VPRM-trained models (Qwen2.5-7B-GRPO-VPRM) achieve 75.0% Coherent Accuracy (accuracy restricted to coherent instances)
claim c6 [quantitative]: Neural process-reward baselines (Qwen2.5-7B-GRPO-PRM-GPT-OSS) achieve 78.2% Accuracy and 56.1% F1 on CochraneForest, consistently outperformed by VPRM
claim c7 [qualitative]: Under mild reward-separation assumptions (finite variance, correct reasoning receives strictly larger expected reward than incorrect, sufficiently large group size), verifiable process rewards offer theoretical guarantees that gradient-based updates assign positive expected weight to correct reasoning trajectories and negative weight to inconsistent ones
claim c8 [negative]: VPRMs rely on the existence of deterministic, domain-specific rules; tasks lacking well-defined intermediate reasoning steps may not benefit directly. Empirical evaluation is currently focused on risk-of-bias assessment; generalisation to other domains, particularly open-ended reasoning tasks, remains to be established.

Depth-Recurrent Attention Mixtures: Giving Latent Reasoning the Attention it Deserves (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: depth-recurrence, depth-attention, sparse-moe, attention-mixture, latent-reasoning, parameter-sharing
Dreamer is a depth-recurrent Transformer that combines three orthogonal attention mechanisms: sequence attention (SA) over tokens, depth attention (DA) over previous depths of the same token, and expert attention (EA) as a sparse mixture-of-experts. The core single-layer architecture is repeated across depths, with parameter sharing and depth-position-encoded routing via RoPE. Information flows through the depth dimension via DA (addressing hidden-size bottleneck), through experts via sparse EA (addressing layer-size bottleneck), and through sequence via standard SA. DA uses a per-token KV cache that is overwritten after each token, keeping memory overhead constant. Attention mixtures are composed sequentially (DA → SA → EA) or partially parallel, with residuals normalized via RMSNorm applied to the stream itself.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Models require 2 to 8x fewer training tokens for the same accuracy as FLOP-, parameter-, and memory-matched SOTA
claim c2 [qualitative]: DR+DA with depth 16 outperforms LA with depth 32 on all reasoning benchmarks
claim c3 [qualitative]: Depth-recurrent models use 2 to 11x larger expert selection diversity than SOTA MoEs
claim c4 [quantitative]: Global expert balancing achieves Gini coefficient of ca. 0.075, indicating strong load balancing across depths
claim c5 [qualitative]: At depth 16: DR achieves 4.0x data efficiency; DR+DA achieves 2.9x data efficiency (average across benchmarks)
claim c6 [qualitative]: At depth 32: DR achieves 2.6x data efficiency; DR+DA achieves 3.5x data efficiency (average across benchmarks)
claim c7 [qualitative]: Depth attention enables deeper models to scale more effectively than shallow models; DA particularly alleviates hidden-size bottleneck which becomes more pronounced as depth increases and more reasoning shifts from sequence to depth dimension
claim c8 [qualitative]: Depth attention patterns show nontrivial routing: early depths look back to first few depths; middle depths are recalled later; final output informed by middle to high depths—extending expressivity beyond uniform skip-connections

Thinking Broad, Acting Fast: Latent Reasoning Distillation from Multi-Perspective Chain-of-Thought for E-Commerce Relevance (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: multi-perspective-cot, knowledge-distillation, dpo-preference-learning, latent-reasoning-alignment, e-commerce-relevance
A two-stage framework for e-commerce relevance ranking: (1) Multi-Perspective CoT (MPCoT) teacher, a Qwen3-14B LLM fine-tuned via SFT on aggregated rationales from three reasoning perspectives (User Intent, Structured Analysis, Business Rules), then refined with DPO on conflicting cases to learn perspective selection. (2) Latent Reasoning Knowledge Distillation (LRKD) student, a BERT-multilingual-base cross-encoder augmented with a lightweight trainable latent reasoning extractor that maps BERT hidden states to a latent vector, aligned via MSE loss to frozen sentence embeddings of teacher CoTs, enabling compact deployment without rationale generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our final teacher model (MPCoT SFT+DPO) surpasses the strongest single-perspective baseline by an average of +1.96 points in accuracy and +3.5 points in F1 across all datasets.
claim c2 [quantitative]: The multi-perspective oracle (three prompts with different perspectives, correct if any matches) achieved 77.52% accuracy, significantly outperforming the pass@3 single-perspective control (72.59%) and the single User Intent perspective (64.61%).
claim c3 [quantitative]: LRKD with GAT-based extractor improves accuracy by +2.37 points and F1 by +3.91 points on average over BERT-multilingual-base baseline.
claim c4 [quantitative]: LRKD with GAT outperforms MKD (Zhao et al., 2025) by +1.15 points in accuracy and +2.49 points in F1 across datasets.
claim c5 [quantitative]: Online A/B test on AliExpress search advertising platform: +1.42% improvement in Revenue Per Mille (RPM), +0.48% increase in Click-Through Rate (CTR), and +0.4% increase in relevance satisfaction (RS) score.
claim c6 [quantitative]: The latent reasoning vector achieves an average F1 score of 0.403 on predicting top 15 non-trivial CoT keywords, an 81.8% relative improvement over [CLS] representation (0.221).
claim c7 [quantitative]: Teacher model inference latency is 46,800 ms vs. student BERT baseline 132.22 ms and LRKD-GAT 148.76 ms (marginal +16.54 ms overhead) on NVIDIA A100 GPU for 100 query–item pairs per batch.
claim c8 [negative]: Removing the latent reasoning extractor (replacing with [CLS] representation) degrades performance by −1.49 to −1.73 accuracy points and −0.89 to −1.35 F1 points, indicating representation overload when a single vector must capture both classification and reasoning.

FunPRM: Function-as-Step Process Reward Model with Meta Reward Correction for Code Generation (2026)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, chain-of-function, meta-learning, reward-correction, best-of-n-selection, code-generation
FunPRM is a process reward model (PRM) for code generation that operates in two stages: (1) a Chain-of-Function prompting strategy guides the base LLM to generate modular code organized into functions, with each function treated as a reasoning step for PRM evaluation; (2) a meta-learning-based reward correction mechanism trains a lightweight correction table to denoise Monte Carlo-estimated partial-solution rewards using clean final-solution rewards obtained from unit-test evaluation, then uses gradient-based meta-optimization to refine the partial rewards such that the updated PRM generalizes better to clean final-solution data.
claim c1 [quantitative]: FunPRM achieves state-of-the-art performance on LiveCodeBench with 80.9 pass@1 when combined with OpenAI O4-mini (High)
claim c2 [qualitative]: FunPRM outperforms baselines across all four base LLMs (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-Coder-33B, Qwen2.5-7B-Coder) and achieves best overall performance in 18 out of 20 settings on LiveCodeBench and BigCodeBench subsets
claim c3 [qualitative]: On LiveCodeBench (2025-02-01 present) with O4-mini (High), FunPRM achieves 100% on Easy (31 problems), 92.3% on Medium (39 problems), and 63.9% on Hard (61 problems)
claim c4 [qualitative]: FunPRM surpasses Self-Certainty baseline, indicating the necessity of a learned reward model compared to simpler heuristic-based test-time scaling strategies, and outperforms outcome reward model (ORM)-based scaling, highlighting the advantage of decomposing code generation into intermediate steps and assigning rewards at a finer granularity
claim c5 [qualitative]: In human evaluation on 100 problems where both FunPRM and baseline produce correct solutions and 100 where both produce incorrect solutions, FunPRM is consistently preferred over the base model (Qwen2.5-7B-Coder) for code readability (correct solutions) and reusability/ease of fixing (incorrect solutions)
claim c6 [qualitative]: FunPRM continues to improve the performance of the base LLM (Qwen2.5-7B-Coder) through test-time scaling on HumanEval+ and MBPP+ without additional training, demonstrating domain generalization capability
claim c7 [qualitative]: Ablation study on LiveCodeBench (2025-02-01 present) shows FunPRM full model achieves 80.9% overall, FunPRM without Chain-of-Function achieves 80.1%, and FunPRM without meta reward correction achieves 80.2%
claim c8 [qualitative]: As problem difficulty increases on LiveCodeBench, the distribution of the number of PRM steps (generated functions) generally increases, indicating harder problems typically require longer chains of logic, though some hard problems are solved using only a single function

ReGuLaR: Variational Latent Reasoning Guided by Rendered Chain-of-Thought (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vae-latent-reasoning, visual-text-compression, kl-regularization, rendered-guidance, multi-modal-reasoning, compression-efficiency
ReGuLaR formulates latent reasoning within a Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE) framework, where an LLM samples latent reasoning states from a posterior distribution conditioned on previous states and a question. During training, explicit reasoning chains are rendered as images; a frozen visual encoder (DeepSeek-OCR) extracts dense visual-semantic representations from these rendered images, which regularize the posterior distribution via a learned adapter. The LLM's latent reasoning head generates mean and variance for each latent state; the language head decodes final answers from the latent sequence. Only the latent reasoning head, adapter, and LoRA modules are trained; the backbone LLM and visual encoder remain frozen.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ReGuLaR achieves 45.6% accuracy on GSM8K-Aug while reducing average reasoning length to 3.03 steps, compared to CoLaR's 41.7% accuracy at 4.70 steps.
claim c2 [quantitative]: ReGuLaR reduces average reasoning length by approximately 35% compared to CoLaR (from 4.70 to 3.03) while delivering substantial accuracy gains across all datasets.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GSM-Hard with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, ReGuLaR achieves higher accuracy than CoLaR (which requires 12.8 reasoning steps) with only 3.1 steps.
claim c4 [quantitative]: In extreme compression (single latent reasoning state) on MATH, ReGuLaR improves average accuracy from 7.76% to 11.9% while reducing reasoning length from 62.2 to 1.00 steps.
claim c5 [qualitative]: On molecular captioning with LLaMA-1B, ReGuLaR achieves state-of-the-art performance across BLEU-2, BLEU-4, METEOR, and ROUGE metrics with a single latent reasoning step, outperforming explicit CoT which uses hundreds of reasoning steps.
claim c6 [qualitative]: ReGuLaR demonstrates consistent positive scaling behavior across model scales (1B to 8B), maintaining significant performance margins over CoLaR and Coconut across all model sizes and datasets.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Compared to token grouping strategies in CoLaR, ReGuLaR's visual-text rendering preserves semantic integrity and provides better regularization with less information loss.
claim c8 [qualitative]: ReGuLaR natively supports multi-modal reasoning by rendering and encoding non-textual elements (charts, graphs, diagrams) alongside text within latent reasoning processes, maintaining standard textual input-output interface.

Faithful-Patchscopes: Understanding and Mitigating Model Bias in Hidden Representations Explanation of Large Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: patchscopes, activation-patching, logit-recalibration, debiasing, interpretability, contrastive-decoding
The paper studies unfaithfulness in Patchscopes, a framework that uses LLMs themselves to decode and explain hidden representations by patching internal activations into a target prompt. The authors identify that model bias from imbalanced linguistic patterns causes LLMs to override contextual information in patched representations during generation. They propose BALOR (Bias Alignment through Logit Recalibration), an inference-time decoding method that contrasts logits from an unpatched contrastive prompt against logits from a patched target prompt, recalibrating the logit distribution to suppress bias and amplify contextual information without modifying model parameters.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Faithfulness of Patchscopes decreases by 11.84% to 28.47% under biased linguistic patterns, with an average 18.84% decrease
claim c2 [quantitative]: Imbalanced linguistic patterns strongly predict model bias: every standard deviation increase in frequency difference increases odds of bias by a factor of 9.338
claim c3 [quantitative]: BALOR achieves up to 33% relative performance improvement over vanilla Patchscopes
claim c4 [qualitative]: BALOR Show Rate (SR) in zero-shot color task: Llama2-7b 49.39%, Llama3.2-1b 71.66%, Llama3-8b 56.42%, Qwen3-4b 73.45% (BALOR Shared mode)
claim c5 [qualitative]: BALOR Show Rate (SR) in few-shot color task: Llama2-7b 52.10%, Llama3.2-1b 69.77%, Llama3-8b 47.62%, Qwen3-4b 68.90% (BALOR Shared mode)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Model bias arises from imbalanced linguistic patterns: logistic regression correlation (Spearman ρ) between logit difference and gradient similarity alignment shows weak correlation (p=0.001), indicating complementary metrics
claim c7 [qualitative]: BALOR Shared mode is stable across sampling temperatures (0.0 to 1.6) and hyperparameter α values, consistently outperforming vanilla Patchscopes; Divided mode exhibits higher variability
claim c8 [negative]: Prompt-based debiasing methods (Closed-Book, Internal-Evidence, Data-Bound) show unstable and inconsistent performance across tasks; fine-tuning-based methods (SFT) degrade substantially when biased training data conflicts with evaluation data

Lookahead-then-Verify: Reliable Constrained Decoding for Diffusion LLMs under Context-Free Grammars (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, grammar-enforcement, lookahead-verify, cfg-validation, diffusion-lm, non-autoregressive, recovery-mechanism
LAVE is a constrained decoding system for diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) that enforces context-free grammar (CFG) compliance during non-autoregressive generation. The core mechanism is lookahead-then-verify: when the dLLM proposes a token for a masked position in an incomplete prefix, LAVE samples multiple complete prefixes by filling remaining masked positions using the model's predicted token distributions. These sampled prefixes are validated against the CFG using a grammar parser (Earley or LR). A token is accepted only if at least one sampled complete prefix can be extended into a valid sentence under the grammar. If proposal fails repeatedly (exceeding an attempt budget), a cache-enhanced recovery mechanism resets to a previously verified prefix and generates an additional token sampled from the set of grammar-admissible successors, preserving both non-autoregressive behavior and constraint reliability.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Current approaches specifically designed for dLLMs may allow intermediate outputs that are impossible to complete into valid sentences, which significantly limits their reliability in practice.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Dream-v0-Instruct-7B exhibits a syntax error rate of up to 23.8% on HumanEval-CPP.
claim c3 [quantitative]: With a lookahead size of 10, the acceptance rate reaches 98.1% for Dream-v0-Instruct-7B and 97.3% for LLaDA-8B-Instruct in preliminary experiments validating lookahead-then-verify feasibility.
claim c4 [quantitative]: On SMILES-Bench with LLaDA-8B, LAVE raises syntactic@1 from 66.0% to 98.7%, syntactic@3 from 74.1% to 99.4%, syntactic@5 from 75.9% to 100.0%, and syntactic@10 from 78.9% to 100.0%.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On CPP-Bench with DiffuC-7B, LAVE increases syntactic@1 from 79.9% to 97.0%, syntactic@3 from 80.2% to 99.1%, syntactic@5 from 80.5% to 99.6%, and syntactic@10 from 81.7% to 100.0%.
claim c6 [quantitative]: On CPP-Bench with Dream-7B, LAVE outperforms the best baseline IG-CD by 8.4% in terms of syntactic@1 (96.0% vs 87.6%).
claim c7 [qualitative]: LAVE consistently outperforms existing baselines and achieves substantial improvements in syntactic correctness across four widely used dLLMs (LLaDA-8B, LLaDA-1.5, Dream-7B, DiffuC-7B) and three representative benchmarks (CPP-Bench, JSON-Bench, SMILES-Bench), while incurring negligible runtime overhead.
claim c8 [qualitative]: LAVE benefits more from increasing k than FS-CD. On CPP-Bench with LLaDA-1.5, as k increases from 1 to 10, LAVE improves syntactic@k from 91.3% to 98.8%, whereas FS-CD shows only a marginal increase from 73.8% to 74.2%.

MeKi: Memory-based Expert Knowledge Injection for Efficient LLM Scaling (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: memory-lookup, knowledge-injection, reparameterization, low-rank-gating, rom-offloading, sparse-activation
MeKi augments each Transformer layer with a parallel memory-based expert module that retrieves token-level knowledge vectors from a layer-specific embedding table and injects them into the hidden state via low-rank gated fusion. During training, a non-linear projection (SwiGLU) synthesizes dynamic features from global word embeddings; these projections are re-parameterized post-training into static lookup tables offloaded to ROM, eliminating inference-time FLOPs while preserving parameter count in RAM.
claim c1 [quantitative]: MeKi-1.7B achieves an average zero-shot benchmark score of 59.7, matching the 4B dense model's score of 60.5, while maintaining a 2.26x advantage in decoding speed
claim c2 [quantitative]: On ARC-Challenge, MeKi-1.7B achieves 37.9, surpassing the 1.7B baseline by +3.5 points and matching the 4B baseline (38.0)
claim c3 [quantitative]: On SciQ, MeKi-1.7B reaches 85.4, outperforming the baseline by 4.8 points
claim c4 [quantitative]: MeKi-1.7B outperforms PLE by 2.7 points and Engram by 1.8 points on average zero-shot performance
claim c5 [qualitative]: MeKi maintains the same generation speed (token/s) as the dense baseline on Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite with KV cache length 10K
claim c6 [qualitative]: Re-parameterization strategy merges dynamic projections into static lookup tables, achieving zero inference latency overhead by offloading knowledge to ROM
claim c7 [qualitative]: MeKi exhibits systematically lower KL divergence across all layers compared to baseline, indicating accelerated prediction convergence toward final output distribution
claim c8 [qualitative]: Placing MeKi parallel to FFN achieves best performance; positioning after Attention or FFN leads to information bottleneck and performance drop

Scaling Agentic Verifier for Competitive Coding (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: test-time-scaling, execution-based-verification, multi-turn-agentic-reasoning, input-generation, rejection-fine-tuning, rl-optimization, best-of-k-reranking
Agentic Verifier is an LLM-based agent that iteratively generates discriminative test inputs for competitive programming code verification. The system interacts multi-turn with a code execution sandbox, executing candidate solution pairs on proposed inputs, observing execution feedback and behavioral outputs, and refining a hypothesis about distinguishing input generators. After multi-turn reasoning, it outputs a final input generator program. The verifier is trained via rejection fine-tuning on successful trajectories from a teacher model, then further optimized with GRPO reinforcement learning using sparse outcome-based rewards (validity + discriminativeness). At test time, pairs of candidate solutions are ranked by output agreement on verifier-generated test inputs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Ground-truth test inputs consistently outperform randomly generated inputs by a large margin on USACO, with random generation failing to close the performance gap even at scale
claim c2 [quantitative]: Agentic Verifier achieves up to +10–15% absolute gains in Best@K accuracy compared to execution-based baselines
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 + USACO, Agentic Verifier achieves 73.1% Best@8 (+10.3 over Vanilla) and 74.5% Best@64 (+11.7 over Vanilla)
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507 + CodeForces, Agentic Verifier achieves 49.9% Best@8 (+10.2 over Vanilla) and 50.9% Best@64 (+11.2 over Vanilla)
claim c5 [quantitative]: The 30B trained verifier outperforms the zero-shot 235B model by a large margin on Best@8 (82.4% vs 79.5% on LiveCodeBench, 47.3% vs 43.2% on OJBench)
claim c6 [quantitative]: On OJBench hard problems with Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507, Agentic Verifier achieves 44.0% Best@8 compared to 24.7% for Random Generator baseline, demonstrating +19.3 point advantage
claim c7 [qualitative]: Agentic Verifier exhibits stronger and more stable scaling trends as the number of test inputs increases compared to baseline methods, which show earlier saturation and diminishing marginal improvements
claim c8 [qualitative]: Benchmark test suites act as imperfect verifiers covering only a limited portion of the valid input space, leading to false positives; the agentic verifier can expose hidden behavioral discrepancies by discovering counterexamples

Guided Verifier: Collaborative Multimodal Reasoning via Dynamic Process Supervision (2026)
seam: text; tags: process-supervision, verifier-loop, dynamic-guidance, rl-alignment, multimodal-reasoning, error-correction, closed-loop-control
Guided Verifier is a dual-agent framework for multimodal reasoning that transforms open-loop policy rollouts into closed-loop collaborative inference. A frozen verifier (trained on CoRe dataset) observes each policy-generated reasoning step in real-time, assigns a hallucination score and corrective guidance, and feeds this guidance back into the policy's next-step context. The policy (Qwen3-VL-8B) is then trained via Guided-GRPO, a variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization that optimizes a composite reward signal (correctness, hallucination penalty, format compliance) under the verifier's dynamic supervision.
claim c1 [quantitative]: An 8B-parameter model trained via Guided-GRPO achieves 51.07% accuracy on MathVerse (Test-mini), outperforming larger open-source baselines and rivaling proprietary systems.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On MathVista, Guided-GRPO achieves 77.88% accuracy (GPS), 76.51% (ALG), 72.11% (Val), compared to base model 67.31%, 64.06%, 62.44% respectively.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On MMMU, Guided-GRPO achieves 72.11% accuracy, compared to base model 62.44%.
claim c4 [quantitative]: The CoRe dataset synthesis pipeline produces 2,792 trajectories with 26,360 step-wise supervision signals (24,946 positives and 1,406 negatives).
claim c5 [qualitative]: The specialized SFT verifier trained on CoRe significantly reduces the number of interaction turns required to reach a correct solution, typically resolving reasoning paths within 2-3 turns, compared to prolonged circular arguments with a naive verifier.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Simply coupling two models without specific alignment (policy + naive verifier) yields only marginal or negligible gains, confirming that the capability injected by CoRe SFT dataset is the primary driver for enabling effective test-time guidance.
claim c7 [theoretical]: Theorem 3.1 proves that under negligible false rejections, the guided closed-loop framework yields validity probability approximately (1 - ε·δ)^n compared to open-loop (1 - ε)^n, exponentially suppressing error propagation over trajectory length n.
claim c8 [qualitative]: The Guided Verifier framework proves more resource-efficient than blindly scaling model parameters, achieving performance parity with significantly larger models (e.g., QVQ-72B) while utilizing a fraction of the parameter count.

Correctness-Optimized Residual Activation Lens (CORAL): Transferrable and Calibration-Aware Inference-Time Steering (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, probe-based-intervention, calibration-improvement, residual-correctness, distributed-signals
CORAL is an inference-time steering method that trains weight-decay regularized MLP probes on frozen residual stream activations to predict residual correctness (the gap between ideal and actual probability distributions). At inference, the probe's predictions are centered and additively applied to shift base probabilities toward better calibration and accuracy, without modifying model weights.
claim c1 [quantitative]: CORAL consistently improves accuracy by 10% and expected calibration error (ECE) by 50% on average across three 7B-parameter models
claim c2 [quantitative]: CORAL demonstrates transfer to the complete published test sets of four held-out benchmarks (ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, Math-MC, OpenBookQA), averaging 14% accuracy improvements and 49% ECE improvements
claim c3 [quantitative]: On RACE with Deepseek-LLM-7B-Chat, CORAL outperforms the second-best method by 17.39 percentage points in accuracy and achieves 64% relative improvement in ECE
claim c4 [qualitative]: Probe training requires under 5 hours on a single GPU with 8.4k–10k training questions
claim c5 [qualitative]: Middle layers (17–21) of DeepSeek-7B-Chat contain the most useful correctness signals for steering, with layers 17–21 yielding the strongest improvements in both accuracy and calibration
claim c6 [negative]: Individual SAE features produce negligible causal effects on model calibration, with mean ECE change from single-neuron ablation equal to 0.0125 percentage points, and aggregating top features by ablation impact does not improve accuracy or calibration
claim c7 [negative]: Multi-layer CORAL, where probes are trained on concatenated activations from the top 3–5 layers, produced no meaningful improvement over single-layer steering
claim c8 [qualitative]: CORAL's evaluation is limited to multiple-choice question answering, leaving open whether correctness signals generalize to free-form generation where correctness is harder to define

Latent Reasoning with Supervised Thinking States (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: latent-reasoning, recurrent-state, teacher-forcing, kv-injection, chunk-based-processing, speculative-prefill
Thinking States augments a backbone LLM (Qwen2.5) with two lightweight modules: a Thinking Block (single-layer autoregressive Transformer decoder) that generates natural-language reasoning tokens from deep representations, and a Compression Block (Transformer encoder with pooling) that maps variable-length thought sequences into fixed-size states. During inference, the model processes input chunks iteratively: inject the previous state into shallow-layer embeddings, forward through the LLM to deep layers, decode thoughts from deep representations, compress them into a state, and repeat. During training, target reasoning sequences (paired with each chunk) are teacher-forced to inject gold states, enabling fully parallel training without backpropagation through time. States never extend the context window; they condition future processing via residual injection at shallow layers.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On State Tracking Parity task trained on N=10, Thinking States achieves 98.37% accuracy
claim c2 [quantitative]: On 2-Hop QA Full Knowledge, Thinking States achieves 54.91% accuracy with 1.19x speedup vs CoT (54.79% accuracy, 1x speedup)
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GSM8K-style math problems, Thinking States achieves 42.22% accuracy with 2.66x speedup vs CoT (60.50% accuracy, 1x speedup)
claim c4 [quantitative]: Thinking States outperforms Coconut on GSM by approximately 10 percentage points (42.22% vs 32.65%)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Length generalization on Parity: trained on N≤10, Thinking States achieves 100% accuracy on N=40, vs CoT 64.38%
claim c6 [qualitative]: Training cost comparison: BPTT incurs linearly increasing wall-clock time with recurrent steps, whereas Thinking States maintains close to constant training time due to teacher forcing, preventing cost penalties at 10+ steps
claim c7 [qualitative]: Approximately 12% of queries correctly solved by Thinking States are not solved by CoT, with CoT exhibiting hallucinated reasoning steps or overly complex intermediate computations
claim c8 [negative]: State ambiguity: Thinking States fails when the quantity of interest is specified only in the final clause of a query; prepending the question to the start of the prompt zero-shot improves accuracy from 42.22% to 48.65% on error cases, suggesting limitation stems from causal processing order rather than the mechanism itself

SecCodePRM: A Process Reward Model for Code Security (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: step-level-reward, process-reward-model, code-security, vulnerability-detection, safe-generation, inference-scaling
SecCodePRM is a process reward model that assigns context-aware, step-level security scores to code trajectories. It operates at the granularity of discrete code steps (separated by double newlines), evaluates partial and complete code by conditioning each step's prediction on preceding context, and uses a classification head to produce a sequence of security probabilities. The model learns from step-level supervision labels derived from static analyzers (e.g., CodeQL) and expert annotations. Information flows from code input → step tokenization → transformer backbone (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B) → classification head → per-step safety logit margins → aggregated or ranked for either full-code vulnerability detection (risk-sensitive aggregation) or safe code generation (inference-time scaling via candidate ranking).
claim c1 [quantitative]: SecCodePRM outperforms LLMxCPG on SVEN by a margin of 12%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On PrimeVul paired evaluation, SecCodePRM achieves 93.66% Pair-wise Correct Prediction (P-C) with 0% Pair-wise Vulnerable (P-V) and 0% Pair-wise Reversed (P-R)
claim c3 [quantitative]: On PrimeVul full-code classification, SecCodePRM achieves 96.83% accuracy and 96.73 F1, compared to 72.50% accuracy and 62.06 F1 for LLMxCPG
claim c4 [quantitative]: On PreciseBugs (20,000+ token examples), SecCodePRM achieves 0.59 F1, outperforming MSIVD (0.48 F1) and other baselines
claim c5 [quantitative]: On partial-code vulnerability detection, SecCodePRM achieves 100.00 F1 on PreciseBugs and maintains robust F1 across SVEN, PrimeVul, and ReposVul, outperforming models up to 17 times its size
claim c6 [quantitative]: On safe code generation with CWEval, SecCodePRM achieves 11.09% increase on func@1 and 12.98% increase on safe@1 compared to baseline
claim c7 [qualitative]: SecCodePRM accuracy remains stable even as token counts increase significantly, mitigating the 'lost in the middle' or signal-decay issues common in traditional sequence classification models
claim c8 [qualitative]: On LiveCodeBench general competitive programming, SecCodePRM mostly maintains or even improves general functionality, with QC2.5-32B achieving 55.19% pass@1, outperforming both probability-based ranking (53.23%) and QwenPRM (53.23%), suggesting security and functionality are synergistic dimensions in the coding domain

GTS: Inference-Time Scaling of Latent Reasoning with a Learnable Gaussian Thought Sampler (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-its, learned-perturbation, gaussian-policy, grpo-training, inference-time-scaling
GTS is a lightweight, learnable module that augments frozen latent reasoning backbones (COCONUT, CODI) by predicting context-dependent Gaussian perturbation distributions over hidden states at each reasoning step. During inference, perturbations are sampled via reparameterization and injected into latent trajectories. The backbone remains frozen; only the sampler (mean and log-std heads) is trained via GRPO-style policy optimization using task rewards (correctness + confidence shaping). Information flow: input → frozen backbone generates deterministic hidden states → GTS predicts conditional Gaussian parameters → perturbations sampled and added to states → perturbed states fed back into backbone for subsequent steps → final answer decoded greedily.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Stronger perturbations do not necessarily yield better sampling quality: they often induce larger distribution shifts without producing more useful reasoning paths or better final decisions.
claim c2 [qualitative]: On COCONUT, mild dropout (p=0.1) achieves positive sampling gain (SG) with minimal JS divergence, whereas stronger dropout (p=0.5) leads to negative SG despite increased divergence.
claim c3 [qualitative]: GTS achieves stronger scaling behavior than dropout-based sampling and standard Gaussian noise baselines as sampling budget increases on both COCONUT and CODI.
claim c4 [qualitative]: GTS avoids under-exploration and over-exploration regimes: it achieves positive sampling gain with relatively controlled distribution shift, whereas heuristic methods do not control this trade-off explicitly.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Dense reward (correctness + confidence shaping) consistently outperforms accuracy-only reward for GTS training, with the performance gap widening as sampling budget increases.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Step-wise signal-to-noise ratio analysis reveals that GTS does not impose a fixed stochastic schedule; instead, the balance between deterministic and stochastic components adapts to the latent reasoning dynamics of the underlying backbone.
claim c7 [negative]: Empirical scope remains limited to relatively short, answer-focused math tasks and does not yet cover more open-ended, long-form, or non-mathematical reasoning settings.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Out-of-distribution results on MultiArith, SVAMP, and GSM8K-Hard show that GTS remains more consistent than heuristic perturbations without additional training or hyperparameter tuning.

Vectorizing the Trie: Efficient Constrained Decoding for LLM-based Generative Retrieval on Accelerators (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, sparse-matrix-ops, prefix-tree-flattening, tpu-gpu-native, vectorized-masking, beam-search
STATIC is a constrained decoding system for LLM-based generative retrieval that flattens prefix trees (tries) into Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) matrices, transforming irregular tree traversals into vectorized sparse matrix operations executable on TPUs/GPUs. The system maintains a transition state vector tracking position in the prefix tree for each beam, uses a dense tensor mask for early decoding layers (up to layer L_d), and applies a Vectorized Node Transition Kernel (VNTK) for deeper layers. Logits are converted to log-probabilities, masked against valid token transitions, and passed to beam search with constrained selection. The architecture is branch-free and fully static, enabling XLA/Inductor compilation without CPU callbacks.
claim c1 [quantitative]: STATIC achieves latency overhead of 0.033 ms per decoding step, representing 0.25% of total inference time on a 3 billion parameter model
claim c2 [quantitative]: STATIC achieves 948x speedup over a CPU trie implementation
claim c3 [qualitative]: STATIC achieves 47-1033x speedup over hardware-accelerated binary-search baseline (PPV)
claim c4 [quantitative]: STATIC requires approximately 0.4 MB of memory for every 1 million restricted vocabulary items; for million-item constraint set, maximal HBM usage is 200 GB (tightenable to 100 GB)
claim c5 [qualitative]: In online A/B test on YouTube Home Feed with 7-day freshness constraint: 7-day fresh video views increased by [value not stated in extracted text], 3-day fresh views increased by [value not stated], click-through rate increased by [value not stated], and strategic user segment satisfaction improved by [value not stated]
claim c6 [qualitative]: STATIC maintains constant latency with respect to SID vocabulary size across practical ranges (256 to 32k tokens tested)
claim c7 [qualitative]: I/O complexity of STATIC scales linearly with constraint set size, contrasting with logarithmic scaling of binary-search-based methods
claim c8 [negative]: STATIC requires precomputing and storing dense tensor masks for early decoding layers; construction becomes prohibitively expensive for layers beyond L_d due to exponential growth of vocabulary space

Step-Level Sparse Autoencoder for Reasoning Process Interpretation (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, step-level-reasoning, context-conditioned, information-bottleneck, incremental-features, linear-probing, reasoning-verification
SSAE is a context-conditioned sparse autoencoder that operates at the step level of reasoning trajectories. It consists of a context-conditioned encoder (Transformer) that maps concatenated context and step to a dense embedding, a sparse projector that expands this embedding into a high-dimensional sparse latent space with controlled sparsity, and a context-conditioned decoder (Transformer) that reconstructs the step by integrating latent features with contextual embeddings. The key mechanism is an information bottleneck formed by sparsity constraints, which forces the latent vector to encode only incremental information relative to prior context, squeezing out background information. A dynamic weight controller automatically adjusts the sparsity regularization coefficient during training to maintain target sparsity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SSAE features can predict step correctness with at least 10% accuracy increase compared with naive majority-class prediction, achieving 78.58% on GSM8K and 82.74% on MATH-500 for SSAE-Qwen
claim c2 [quantitative]: SSAE features can almost perfectly predict step length with Step Length Error of 2.10 on GSM8K and 1.94 on MATH-500 for SSAE-Qwen
claim c3 [quantitative]: SSAE features predict first token perplexity with 4.09 on GSM8K and 1.46 on MATH-500 for SSAE-Qwen, compared to baseline of 61.01 and 74.96 respectively
claim c4 [quantitative]: N2G pattern extraction for SSAE-Qwen achieves F1 score of 79.49 on GSM8K with sparsity target of 10, compared to 61.03 at sparsity 5 and 52.73 at sparsity 3
claim c5 [quantitative]: Probe-Guided weighted voting strategy achieves 46.80% on GSM8K for Qwen2.5-0.5B, compared to Self-Consistency baseline of 46.20%
claim c6 [quantitative]: Latent-swap test shows decoded content semantic independence score of 4.96 out of 5 for SSAE-Qwen on GSM8K with observed agreement of 94.87% and Gwet's AC1 of 0.96 across multiple LLM judges
claim c7 [qualitative]: Token-based SAEs fail to capture step-level information such as reasoning direction and semantic transitions, with performance similar to statistical baselines, whereas SSAE achieves accurate prediction of these properties
claim c8 [qualitative]: SSAE disentangles shared dimensions (encoding surface-level stylistic attributes like lexical choice and syntax) from unique dimensions (encoding deep reasoning-specific attributes like order of operations and reasoning direction)

The Hidden Cost of Structured Generation in LLMs: Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding (2026)
seam: text; tags: constrained-decoding, draft-then-constrain, test-time-composition, kl-projection, structured-generation
Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding (DCCD) is a two-stage, training-free inference procedure that decouples semantic planning from structural enforcement. Stage 1 generates an unconstrained draft (free-form reasoning or semantic plan) from a draft model. Stage 2 applies constrained decoding (token masking and renormalization via a constraint oracle) conditioned on the draft context, using a projector model (which may be the same as the draft model or smaller). The draft is concatenated into the context before the constrained decoding step, increasing feasible mass (probability assigned to valid tokens) before hard constraints are enforced. The final output is guaranteed to satisfy the structural constraint while the draft conditioning reduces constraint-induced distortion.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DCCD improves strict structured accuracy by up to +24 percentage points over standard constrained decoding
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K with a 1B model, DCCD improves strict structured accuracy from 15.24% to 39.04%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GSM8K with a 1.5B model, DCCD improves from 49.36% to 73.92%
claim c4 [quantitative]: On MATH500, a DCCD composition (3B total) achieves 12.7 accuracy per billion parameters, compared to 3.6 for an 8B model using constrained decoding, a 253% efficiency improvement
claim c5 [quantitative]: On GSM8K, DCCD improves from 78% at n=1 to 83% at n=13, while constrained decoding improves from 64% to 73%
claim c6 [quantitative]: Decode-time constrained decoding costs 8–20 percentage points in answer accuracy across six model sizes (1B–14B), with the largest drops on smaller models (e.g., 15 points for 1.5B model)
claim c7 [theoretical]: Constrained decoding induces a cumulative reverse-KL projection tax across token positions, with per-step distortion KL(p_base || p_constrained) = -log(feasible_mass), and sequence-level distortion summing to KL(π_base || π_CD) = Σ_t -log(m_t(x₁:t-₁))
claim c8 [qualitative]: DCCD enables parameter-efficient model composition where smaller model pairs match or exceed much larger constrained baselines

Token-Oriented Object Notation vs JSON: A Benchmark of Plain and Constrained Decoding Generation (2026)
seam: text; tags: constrained-decoding, structured-generation, in-context-learning, repair-loops, format-comparison
This is a benchmark paper, not a model architecture paper. It compares three LLM output generation approaches: (1) plain JSON generation via standard sampling, (2) JSON with constrained decoding (structured output), and (3) TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) format with one-shot in-context learning. Information flows from LLM completion → validation pipeline → repair loop (up to 3 cycles) with error text re-injected as context. The paper evaluates 21 models across 4 test cases (users, order, company, invoice) measuring one-shot accuracy, final accuracy, and token consumption.
claim c1 [qualitative]: TOON shows promising accuracy/token consumption ratio for in-domain generation tasks, though this advantage is often reduced by the 'prompt tax' of instructional overhead in shorter contexts.
claim c2 [quantitative]: For TOON-aligned cases (users, order, invoice), TOON achieved 90.5% one-shot accuracy in the users case while using 22% fewer tokens than plain JSON.
claim c3 [quantitative]: In the users case, JSON-SO (Structured Output) proved to be the absolute token efficiency winner at 556 tokens vs TOON 840 tokens.
claim c4 [quantitative]: NousResearch/Hermes-4-405B dropped from 92.5% one-shot accuracy in plain JSON to 35.0% in JSON-SO (constrained decoding).
claim c5 [quantitative]: For Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-fast, plain JSON generation achieved 0% accuracy consuming 37,705 tokens, while JSON-SO achieved 75% accuracy with 4,440 tokens.
claim c6 [negative]: In the company case (non-aligned scenario with deep nesting), TOON achieved 0% one-shot accuracy, while JSON achieved 18.6% and JSON-SO achieved 21.9%.
claim c7 [qualitative]: TOON's true efficiency potential likely follows a non-linear curve, shining only beyond a specific point where cumulative syntax savings of large datasets amortize the initial prompt overhead.
claim c8 [negative]: For simple structures, constrained decoding (JSON-SO) outperformed TOON, hinting that TOON enforcing via frameworks such as xgrammar may not yield the desired results.

SPOT: Span-level Pause-of-Thought for Efficient and Interpretable Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, span-compression, optimal-transport, frozen-head-decoding, interpretability, cot-distillation
SPOT compresses explicit chain-of-thought reasoning into compact latent <pause> tokens that carry intermediate computation in hidden space. The system uses a teacher–student setup with a frozen pretrained LM backbone. During training, explicit reasoning spans are randomly replaced with <pause> tokens, and the student's <pause> hidden state is aligned to the full span of teacher states via Sinkhorn optimal transport, keeping representations compatible with the frozen LM head for interpretability. At inference, <pause> tokens can be inserted externally at controlled intervals to trade off reasoning length and accuracy without enforcing a fixed template.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SPOT improves accuracy by 2.3 points on average while reducing generated tokens by 37.5%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K, SPOT-stage2 achieves 92.72% accuracy (+3.1 points) while reducing output length by 52.1%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On MATH500, SPOT-stage2 achieves 93.80% accuracy (+1.4 points) while reducing output length by 43.0%
claim c4 [quantitative]: On AIME 2025, SPOT-stage2 achieves 39.33% accuracy (+3.3 points) while generating 15.8% fewer tokens
claim c5 [quantitative]: On GPQA-Diamond (out-of-domain), SPOT-stage2 achieves 54.55% accuracy (+4.5 points) with 49.3% reduction in output length
claim c6 [qualitative]: Sinkhorn-regularized optimal transport alignment objective substantially outperforms endpoint-only (End_KL) and pooled-average (MSE) alternatives, achieving stronger accuracy–length trade-offs across math and science QA benchmarks
claim c7 [qualitative]: Frozen-head decoding constraint keeps latent <pause> states directly decodable under the pretrained LM head, enabling readable keyword interpretations of latent thoughts without training auxiliary probes
claim c8 [qualitative]: Two-stage training (OT alignment followed by rejection-sampled fine-tuning) enables controllable implicit reasoning intensity at inference by externally inserting <pause> tokens without enforcing a fixed response template

Holographic Invariant Storage: Design-Time Safety Contracts via Vector Symbolic Architectures (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vsa, hyperdimensional-computing, safety-signal-recovery, context-drift-mitigation, external-memory, codebook-retrieval, design-time-contract
Holographic Invariant Storage (HIS) is a Vector Symbolic Architecture (VSA) protocol that encodes safety constraints as bipolar hypervectors in high-dimensional space, stores them in external memory, and recovers them via algebraic operations (binding, bundling, cleanup via sign function) when the context window becomes corrupted by accumulated interaction history. Information flows offline: safety constraint is bound with a key to produce an invariant stored externally. Online: current context is encoded as a noise vector, normalized to fixed magnitude, superimposed with the stored invariant, binarized via sign cleanup, and unbound with the original key to recover the safety signal. The recovered vector is then re-injected into context or used as a drift metric (cosine similarity) to trigger re-injection.
claim c1 [theoretical]: Single-signal recovery fidelity converges to 1/√2 ≈ 0.707 regardless of noise depth or content
claim c2 [theoretical]: Continuous-noise robustness follows 2Φ(1/σ) - 1, where Φ is the CDF of the standard normal
claim c3 [theoretical]: Multi-signal capacity degradation scales as √(1/(K+1)) for K bundled safety invariants
claim c4 [quantitative]: Monte Carlo simulation (n=1,000) validates convergence to theoretical bound with mean recovery fidelity 0.706 ± 0.003 (95% CI: [0.700, 0.712])
claim c5 [qualitative]: Pilot behavioral experiment (four LLMs, 2B–7B, 720 trials): HIS re-injection improves safety adherence at 2B scale (effect size d=0.60, p<0.05, 95% CI [0.22, 0.98]), with refusal rate improvement matching a matched-frequency timer baseline
claim c6 [negative]: At 7B model scale, all safety intervention conditions saturate to ceiling (all conditions 100% refusal rate), indicating the evaluation is insufficiently challenging for well-aligned models
claim c7 [negative]: HIS does not guarantee that a recovered safety instruction will be obeyed by the model—only that it can be retrieved and re-injected with known fidelity. It does not address distributional shift, reward hacking, or solve the downstream signal utilization problem where the recovered instruction competes for attention weight like any re-injected token sequence.
claim c8 [quantitative]: Computational overhead of HIS restoration protocol is <1 millisecond on CPU (median 0.8 ms over trials for D=8192) with 50-turn integration proof-of-concept achieving 100% codebook retrieval accuracy using a 20-entry safety codebook

From Hallucination to Structure Snowballing: The Alignment Tax of Constrained Decoding in LLM Reflection (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, self-correction, taxonomy-forced, fsm-logits-masking, episodic-memory, reflexion-variant
A three-component self-correction system (Actor, Evaluator, Reflector) deployed on Qwen3-8B. The Actor generates reasoning trajectories; the Evaluator provides binary correctness signals. When a trajectory fails, the Reflector diagnoses errors by outputting a 5-class taxonomy (RETRIEVAL_FOCUS, BRIDGE_FAILURE, HALLUCINATION, INFERENCE_ERROR, FORMATTING_MISMATCH) plus a correction rule. The Reflector uses Outlines-based finite-state-machine constrained decoding to enforce strict JSON schema adherence at the logits level, guaranteeing 100% syntactic compliance without training. Diagnosed errors and corrections are appended to episodic memory, which the Actor reads on subsequent trials.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The application of logic-guided constrained decoding resulted in a marginal but observable performance penalty. The baseline accuracy of 50.0% dropped to 38.0% under constrained conditions.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Models that successfully maintained a correct status across both conditions (the TT group) consumed an average of 2,850 thinking tokens. In contrast, the degraded TF group exhibited a massive token consumption surge, averaging 4,005.5 tokens before ultimately failing.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Out of 100 first-round diagnoses, 96 categorised the failure as a FORMATTING_MISMATCH.
claim c4 [negative]: Simply imposing structural constraints does not improve self-correction performance. Instead, it triggers a new failure mode termed 'structure snowballing,' in which the model becomes trapped in formatting loops and fails to detect or resolve deeper semantic errors.
claim c5 [negative]: Strict constraints are not entirely detrimental; eleven samples successfully recovered from an incorrect baseline state to a correct state under constrained decoding (the FT group). However, twenty-three samples that the model answered correctly in the free-text baseline degraded to incorrect under structural constraints (the TF group).
claim c6 [qualitative]: When the decoding library heavily restricts the output vocabulary, the model shifts its attention weights toward syntactic compliance rather than semantic reasoning. The finite-state machine constraints force the model into 'formatting traps' and 'death loops,' where it achieves near-perfect superficial syntactic alignment yet fails to detect or resolve deeper semantic errors.
claim c7 [qualitative]: The cognitive load required to satisfy strict formatting rules pushes an 8-billion-parameter model into formatting traps. Unguided models default to shallow error correction; when forced into a structured taxonomy, the 8B parameter model lacks the cognitive capacity to dig for deep logical flaws but becomes exceptionally effective at identifying and patching surface-level syntax discrepancies.
claim c8 [theoretical]: These findings expose an 'alignment tax' inherent to constrained decoding, highlighting a tension between structural granularity and internal model capacity in autonomous workflows. Enforcing structured reflection via grammar-constrained decoding introduces an alignment tax when deployed without external training.

Decompose, Look, and Reason: Reinforced Latent Reasoning for VLMs (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, reinforcement-learning, spherical-geometry, multimodal-cot, premise-grounding, policy-gradient, hypersphere-manifold
DLR is a reinforced latent reasoning framework for vision-language models that interleaves three steps: (1) Decompose—the VLM generates textual premises marking what visual details require verification; (2) Look—a visual grounder, conditioned on the premise's hidden state, extracts continuous latent embeddings from image features via cross-attention; (3) Reason—the VLM generates rationales grounded in the injected latents and deduces answers. The framework couples a discrete text policy (VLM) and a continuous latent visual policy (grounder) through joint optimization across three training stages: pretraining for cross-modal alignment via contrastive loss, supervised finetuning to internalize the DLR format, and reinforcement learning with a novel Spherical Gaussian Latent Policy (SGLP) to enable stochastic exploration on the hyperspherical manifold of vision-language features.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DLR achieves 83.8 overall accuracy on V* Bench, outperforming the backbone by 4.2%, LVR by 1.6%, and PixelReasoner by 3.7%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On MathVista, DLR obtains 67.5, exceeding Qwen3-VL baseline by 5.0% and surpassing the strongest open-source baseline LVR by 3.4%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On MMMU-Pro, DLR improves over Qwen3-VL baseline by 5.9%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Removing the latent policy optimization (without ℒ_lat) leads to a drastic performance degradation on MathVista, where the score drops from 67.5% to 57.1%
claim c5 [quantitative]: Removing pretraining hurts performance across all benchmarks; compared with DLR-SFT, the full DLR achieves a 3.3% improvement on V*
claim c6 [qualitative]: DLR provides superior stepwise interpretability through premise-conditioned visual latents, enabling visualization of attention heatmaps that align retrieved latent evidence with image regions relevant to current reasoning subproblems
claim c7 [negative]: Evaluation mainly focuses on vision-centric (image-centric) reasoning benchmarks; generality of the framework to broader multimodal settings such as video reasoning, embodied multimodal decision-making, or tasks requiring richer external interaction remains to be validated
claim c8 [theoretical]: Theorem 3.1 (Latent Policy Effect): By maximizing the latent policy objective, the expected update of the visual grounder increases the alignment between mean direction and latent samples that receive high advantage, encouraging the latent policy to move toward semantically task-relevant directions on the hypersphere

SeLaR: Selective Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models (2026)
seam: text; tags: soft-embeddings, entropy-gating, contrastive-regularization, latent-reasoning, selective-activation, training-free
SeLaR is a training-free latent reasoning framework that selectively activates soft embeddings during language model decoding based on token-level entropy. At each step, the model computes normalized entropy over top-k tokens. If entropy falls below a learned threshold (deterministic step), standard discrete token decoding is used. If entropy exceeds the threshold (exploratory step), a soft embedding is constructed as a probability-weighted mixture of top-k token embeddings and passed to the next layer. An entropy-aware contrastive regularization component then pushes this soft embedding away from the dominant token's direction proportionally to entropy magnitude, sustaining exploration of multiple reasoning paths.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SeLaR achieves the highest average accuracy, improving upon CoT (Sampling) by +0.75%, +3.88%, and +2.45% on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-32B, respectively.
claim c2 [qualitative]: On Qwen3-8B, SeLaR improves AIME 2024 from 76.67% to 83.33% (+6.66%) and AIME 2025 from 66.67% to 80.00% (+13.33%).
claim c3 [quantitative]: Removing selective activation (i.e., applying soft embeddings globally at every step) leads to an average performance drop of 5.19% (from 83.56% to 78.37%).
claim c4 [quantitative]: Removing contrastive regularization results in an average performance drop of 7.82% (from 83.56% to 75.74%), with AIME 2024 dropping from 83.33% to 70.00% and AIME 2025 dropping from 80.00% to 60.00%.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Exploratory steps (high entropy) account for 6.2%–13.8% of total reasoning tokens, averaging 10.0%, confirming that selective activation targets approximately one in ten tokens where exploration is most beneficial.
claim c6 [negative]: SeLaR's TPCA (Tokens per Correct Answer) is higher than CoT on GPQA, attributed to GPQA's knowledge-intensive nature, where latent exploration at uncertain decoding steps provides little benefit when correct answers depend on domain recall rather than multi-step reasoning.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Logit lens analysis shows that without contrastive regularization, the forward pass progressively collapses onto the top-1 trajectory and suppresses the top-2 alternative; with contrastive regularization, both top-1 and top-2 overlaps remain substantially non-zero and comparable in deep layers, confirming genuine sustained exploration of latent reasoning paths.
claim c8 [negative]: SeLaR yields larger improvements on base models with higher confidence (e.g., Qwen3-8B) than on those with lower confidence (e.g., DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B). Less confident models exhibit higher entropy more frequently, triggering excessive exploratory steps.

Schema Key Wording as an Instruction Channel in Structured Generation under Constrained Decoding (2026)
seam: text; tags: constrained-decoding, structured-generation, schema-as-instruction, multi-channel-prompting, json-schema, instruction-placement
The paper studies structured generation under constrained decoding, where LLMs produce JSON outputs that satisfy a predefined schema. The core mechanism treats schema-key wording as an implicit instruction channel: keys like 'step_by_step_reasoning' enter the autoregressive context and condition downstream value generation, separate from and interacting with prompt-level instructions. The system uses a constrained decoding backend (XGrammar) that masks invalid next tokens according to grammar, but the paper argues that schema keys—generated as part of the autoregressive sequence—act as instruction-bearing tokens, not merely neutral field names. Information flow is bidirectional: prompt text and schema keys both influence the model's distribution over continuations, and their effects can be complementary, redundant, or interfering depending on the model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Schema-key wording is not a neutral formatting detail under constrained decoding; changing only the decoder-enforced schema key can noticeably change task accuracy.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Key-only instruction improves Qwen2.5-7B from 79.61 to 86.50 on GSM8K.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Key-only instruction decreases Llama-3.2-3B from 53.15 to 37.38 on GSM8K.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Qwen models tend to benefit more from schema-level instructions, whereas LLaMA models rely more on prompt-level guidance.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Prompt-level and schema-level instructions interact non-additively: the two channels can be complementary, redundant, or interfering rather than simply reinforcing each other.
claim c6 [theoretical]: A CoT-style schema key helps only when its semantic gain exceeds the distortion induced by grammar-constrained projection; this condition is formalized as a sufficient bound derived from Pinsker's inequality on KL divergence between unconstrained and constrained distributions.
claim c7 [negative]: The study focuses only on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K and Math500) and does not cover information extraction, tool use, code generation, data-to-text generation, or agent workflows.
claim c8 [negative]: Model coverage is limited to open-source Qwen and LLaMA families plus one Qwen-based distilled reasoning variant; observed instruction-channel sensitivities may not transfer to other model families, larger systems, or proprietary models.

AgentV-RL: Scaling Reward Modeling with Agentic Verifier (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: verifier-loop, multi-turn-reasoning, tool-use, bidirectional-checking, synthetic-data-gen, reinforcement-learning, test-time-scaling
AgentV-RL is a reward modeling framework that distills a multi-agent verification process into a single LLM. The system coordinates forward and backward agents that perform bidirectional verification: the forward agent traces solutions from premises to conclusions via multi-turn reasoning with tool use (Python interpreter), while the backward agent re-checks conclusions against problem constraints. A synthetic data engine generates verification trajectories, and a two-stage training recipe (rejection fine-tuning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning) trains the verifier to autonomously interleave tool use with internal reasoning for reliable solution assessment.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 79.0% accuracy on MATH500 at Best-of-N (BoN) sampling with N=128, surpassing the previous best outcome-level RM (Skywork-V2-Llama-8B) by 25.2 percentage points
claim c2 [quantitative]: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B reaches 53.3% accuracy on AIME24 at BoN N=128, while all baselines including larger models remain significantly lower
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GSM8K Turn 1, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 94.6% accuracy with a correction rate (C_rate) of 41.6% and degradation rate (D_rate) of 0.6% for iterative refinement
claim c4 [quantitative]: Scaling model size from 0.6B to 4B parameters with Agentic Verifier yields improvement of 14.0 points on MATH500 at BoN N=128 (from 62.2% to 79.0%)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 70.86% on LiveCodeBench and 66.00% on HotpotQA, demonstrating generalization beyond mathematics to competitive code and multihop QA
claim c6 [qualitative]: Agentic Verifier produces 8349 tokens over 11.3 rounds with 1.6 tool calls per verification trajectory, requiring 323.4 seconds latency on A100 GPU (batch size 128), compared to 2560 tokens and 119.0 seconds for the base model
claim c7 [qualitative]: The bidirectional design (forward + backward agents) achieves state-of-the-art performance; ablation shows both forward-only and backward-only variants are competitive but the full design performs best
claim c8 [negative]: The reliance on synthetic, tool-augmented data may not fully represent the variety of real-world reasoning problems, which impede generalization. The multi-turn process increases computational cost, presenting challenges for real-time or resource-limited deployment. Performance is tied to the coverage and reliability of external tools, which can be a bottleneck for certain tasks.

Thinking Without Words: Efficient Latent Reasoning with Abstract Chain-of-Thought (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: discrete-latent-reasoning, abstract-vocabulary, constrained-decoding, policy-iteration, warm-up-rl, information-bottleneck, self-distillation
Abstract Chain-of-Thought augments decoder-only LLMs with a reserved vocabulary of abstract tokens that function as discrete latent reasoning traces. During inference, the model generates a bounded sequence of abstract tokens (instead of natural language CoT) before producing a response. Information flows: prompt → abstract token sequence (constrained decoding) → response. The abstract tokens are trained via a two-stage pipeline: (1) policy iteration warm-up alternating between bottlenecked SFT (where response attends only to prompt and abstract tokens, not verbal CoT) and self-distillation without verbal guidance, and (2) warm-started RL (GRPO with constrained decoding) to refine abstract sequence generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Abstract-CoT achieves up to 11.6× fewer reasoning tokens while demonstrating comparable performance across mathematical reasoning, instruction-following, and multi-hop reasoning
claim c2 [quantitative]: On MATH-500 with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 90.8% accuracy with 144 tokens, compared to SFT + RL baseline of 92.6% accuracy with 1671 tokens
claim c3 [quantitative]: On AlpacaEval with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 60.8 win-rate with 225 tokens, outperforming SFT + RL baseline of 58.4 win-rate with 496 tokens
claim c4 [quantitative]: On HotpotQA with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 58.8 F1 with 171 tokens, compared to SFT + RL baseline of 58.1 F1 with 735 tokens
claim c5 [qualitative]: Abstract-CoT generalizes across model families: on MATH-500, Qwen3-4B achieves 89.8% accuracy with 141 tokens and Granite 4.0 Micro (3B) achieves 74.4% accuracy with 153 tokens, both substantially reducing tokens compared to their verbal CoT baselines
claim c6 [qualitative]: An emergent power law distribution over the abstract vocabulary is observed during RL training, evolving across the training phases, with a single dominant token leveraged across diverse settings and token usage converging to a Zipf-like distribution
claim c7 [negative]: Performing cold-start RL without warm-up is insufficient and often underperforms the base instruction-tuned model; warm-up alone lags behind SFT with verbal CoT on most benchmarks
claim c8 [qualitative]: Permutation ablation shows that abstract sequences exhibit learned compositional behavior: permuting abstract tokens at the token level reduces performance, with smaller performance drops than for verbal CoT permutation, indicating discrete ordering structure in the abstract reasoning language

MoRFI: Monotonic Sparse Autoencoder Feature Identification (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-feature-identification, activation-steering, monotonic-trend-detection, residual-stream-intervention, knowledge-recovery, model-diffing
MoRFI is a two-stage mechanistic interpretability pipeline that identifies SAE latent features causally linked to fine-tuning-induced hallucinations in LLMs. Stage 1 (Algorithm 1) performs bootstrapped monotonic trend detection across a 4D activation tensor (samples × property configurations × latent features × fine-tuning epochs), using Spearman rank and Mann-Kendall tests to isolate features whose activations change monotonically with controlled properties (proportion of unknown facts, training epochs). Stage 2 (Algorithm 2) validates candidate latents through activation steering on a reference task, applying interventions to the residual stream and ranking by downstream performance impact. The pipeline bridges external SAE representations into model activation space via decoder projections, enabling causal attribution of knowledge degradation to sparse latent directions.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Incrementally introducing new knowledge increases hallucinations across Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B and Mistral 7B v03, with the effect being more pronounced with prolonged training.
claim c2 [quantitative]: 69–85% of facts recovered through single-latent steering interventions align with those accessible to a baseline fine-tuned on known facts.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Single-latent interventions sourced with MoRFI substantially outperform steering with the composite direction (difference-in-means SAE activations between known vs. unknown checkpoints).
claim c4 [qualitative]: For the Unknown direction, the best negatively steered latent gives +33.4 accuracy gain while the best positively steered one gives +21.7 for Llama 3.1 8B; for Mistral 7B the difference is +39.77 vs +24.91 and for Gemma 2 9B +18.46 vs +9.03.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Exposure to unknown facts disrupts the model's ability to retrieve stored knowledge along a set of directions in the residual stream; steering experiments suggest forgetting reflects disrupted access rather than erasure of parametric knowledge.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Within-group cosine similarity of top-performing latents is generally low (0.3), indicating that the top-performing latents span a distributed subspace at the middle layer rather than clustering along a shared low-rank structure.
claim c7 [negative]: Steering with control latents that exhibit no significant monotonic trend produces negligible or highly negative accuracy changes, whereas trending latents produce substantial gains, confirming that performance improvements are not artifacts of arbitrary residual stream perturbation.
claim c8 [qualitative]: MoRFI reliably discovers causally relevant latents across distinct models (Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B, Mistral 7B v03), and there is significant overlap between latents surfaced across the Unknown and Epochs dimensions, suggesting they capture a shared signal.

Static Program Slicing Using Language Models With Dataflow-Aware Pretraining and Constrained Decoding (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: seq2seq-code, dfg-pretraining, constrained-decoding, lexical-constraint, syntactic-constraint, tsed-monotonicity, hallucination-prevention
Sliceformer reformulates static program slicing as a sequence-to-sequence task using CodeT5+ (770M parameters). The system consists of three stages: (1) dataflow-aware pretraining on CodeSearchNet, which teaches the model to recognize data dependencies through statement permutation constrained by data-flow graphs and dataflow-guided span corruption at variable and statement granularities; (2) supervised fine-tuning on labeled slicing data from CodeNet-Slice with special control markers; (3) constrained beam search decoding that applies lexical constraints (restricting output vocabulary to input tokens) and syntactic constraints (using Tree Similarity Edit Distance monotonicity to filter structurally invalid candidates at statement boundaries). Information flows from source code + slicing criterion (input tokens) through the encoder-decoder model, then through constrained decoding that reads the original code vocabulary and computes AST edit distances to validate slice structure.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Sliceformer achieves ExactMatch improvements of 6.4% on Java and 21.9% on Python compared to the strongest baseline (NS-slicer with GraphCodeBERT)
claim c2 [quantitative]: On Java programs, Sliceformer attains ExactMatch of 92.20% versus NS-slicer (GraphCodeBERT) at 85.77%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Python programs, Sliceformer reaches ExactMatch of 83.15% compared to NS-slicer (GraphCodeBERT) at 61.25%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Sliceformer outperforms CodeT5+ (SFT) by 5.0% on Java and 5.9% on Python in ExactMatch
